era_context: The year 1400 marks the threshold of the early 15th century, a period of significant transition in world history. In Europe, the Renaissance was beginning to emerge in Italy; the Ottoman Empire was consolidating power in Anatolia and the Balkans; the Ming Dynasty was at its height in China; and the Timurid Empire was fragmenting after Timur's death in 1405. This is an era of cultural flourishing, political consolidation, and the beginning of European overseas expansion.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asian kingdoms (sources exist but underrepresented)
Mesoamerica and South America (Aztec and Inca sources are sparse for this period)

events.title: Owain Glyndŵr's Rebellion Begins (Wales)
events.description: Owain Glyndŵr, a Welsh nobleman, launched a major rebellion against English rule in 1400. The uprising began as a local dispute over land and feudal rights but quickly escalated into a broader Welsh nationalist movement. Glyndŵr declared himself Prince of Wales and sought to establish an independent Welsh state. The rebellion attracted support from Welsh commoners, nobility, and even some English nobles dissatisfied with Henry IV's rule. The conflict would continue for over a decade, making it the last major Welsh uprising against English rule. The rebellion became a symbol of Welsh resistance and national identity.
key_figures: Edmund Mortimer (English nobleman who joined Glyndŵr)

sources.name: English royal records and parliamentary documents

events.certainty_note: The start of Owain Glyndŵr's rebellion in 1400 is well-documented in contemporary English and Welsh sources. The date is firmly established.

events.title: Timur's Invasion of Syria Begins
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the Central Asian conqueror, began his invasion of Syria in late 1400, following his earlier campaigns in Anatolia and the Caucasus. His forces advanced southward, conquering cities and fortifications along the way. The invasion culminated in the sack of Damascus in January 1401. This campaign was part of Timur's broader strategy to consolidate control over the eastern Mediterranean and demonstrate Timurid military supremacy over the Mamluk Sultanate. The invasion marked the peak of Timurid territorial expansion.
events.certainty_note: Timur's invasion of Syria in late 1400 is well-attested in Islamic chronicles. The chronology is established.

events.title: Yongle Emperor Consolidates Ming Power (China)
events.description: The Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di), who seized power in 1402 in a civil war against his nephew, had consolidated his rule by 1400 (using the proleptic Gregorian calendar; the actual date in the Chinese calendar was 1400). By this time, he had eliminated major rivals, established Beijing as the new capital (moving from Nanjing), and begun major construction projects including the Forbidden City. The Yongle Emperor was one of the most powerful and expansionist Ming rulers, and his reign (1402–1424) marked the height of Ming power and influence. He sponsored major naval expeditions, military campaigns, and cultural projects.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di; Ming Dynasty ruler)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Yongle Emperor's consolidation of power is well-documented in the Ming Shilu and other contemporary sources. His reign dates are firmly established.

events.title: Renaissance Begins in Italy (Cultural Movement)
events.description: The Italian Renaissance, a cultural and intellectual movement emphasizing humanism, classical learning, and artistic innovation, was beginning to flourish in the early 1400s. By 1400, major figures such as Petrarch (died 1374) and Boccaccio (died 1375) had already laid the groundwork, and a new generation of humanists, artists, and patrons were emerging. Florence, under the patronage of the Medici family, was becoming a center of artistic and intellectual innovation. The Renaissance would eventually spread throughout Europe and fundamentally transform European culture, art, literature, and thought. The year 1400 is often used as a symbolic starting point for the Renaissance, though the movement developed gradually over the 14th and 15th centuries.
key_figures: Cosimo de' Medici (patron of the arts)

sources.name: Giorgio Vasari, Lives of the Artists (written in the 16th century)

events.certainty_note: The Renaissance was a gradual cultural movement without a precise starting date. The year 1400 is a conventional marker used by historians, but the movement developed over decades. The 'beginning' of the Renaissance is somewhat arbitrary.
cross_references: Contemporary with Ming cultural flourishing in China

events.title: Ottoman Empire Consolidates Anatolia
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under the rule of Mehmed I (r. 1421–1444, but his father Murad II was regent until 1421), was consolidating its control over Anatolia and the Balkans in the early 1400s. By 1400, the Ottomans had conquered much of Anatolia from the Byzantine Empire and various Turkish beyliks (principalities). The Ottoman state was becoming one of the major powers in the eastern Mediterranean and the Balkans. The empire would continue to expand throughout the 15th century, eventually conquering Constantinople in 1453. The consolidation of Ottoman power in Anatolia in the early 1400s was a crucial step in the rise of the Ottoman Empire as a major world power.
key_figures: Murad II (Ottoman Sultan, regent for his young son Mehmed I)
events.certainty_note: Ottoman consolidation of Anatolia in the early 1400s is well-documented in Ottoman and Byzantine sources. The chronology is established.

disconfirming_evidence: The 'beginning' of the Renaissance in 1400 (1400-004) is a historiographic convention rather than a historical fact. Modern scholarship emphasizes that the Renaissance was a gradual cultural movement that developed over the 14th and 15th centuries, with no clear starting point. The idea that the Renaissance 'began' in 1400 is partly a product of 19th-century historiography and should not be taken as a precise historical event. The Ottoman consolidation of Anatolia (1400-005) is sometimes presented as a straightforward process of conquest, but it involved complex negotiations, alliances, and the absorption of existing Turkish principalities; it was not a simple military conquest.
historiographic_note: The year 1400 is moderately well-documented in European, Islamic, and Chinese sources. European sources (English, Italian, Portuguese chronicles) are abundant. Islamic sources (Ottoman and Mamluk chronicles) are available but often written decades after events. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are contemporary and detailed. However, sources from sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are sparse or absent. The surviving sources reflect the interests of literate elites and likely underrepresent the experiences of common people, women, and non-elite groups. The historiography of 1400 is also shaped by the later significance of events that began in this year (e.g., the Renaissance, Ottoman expansion, Ming expansion), which may lead to retrospective overemphasis on their importance at the time.

era_context: The year 1399 falls in the late 14th century, a period of significant political upheaval in Europe, the consolidation of the Ming Dynasty in China, the fragmentation of the Timurid Empire in Central Asia, and the rise of the Ottoman Empire in Anatolia. This is an era of dynastic transitions, cultural flourishing in some regions, and military conflict in others.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asian kingdoms (sources exist but underrepresented)
Mesoamerica and South America (sparse sources for this period)

events.title: Henry IV Deposes Richard II (England)
events.description: Henry Bolingbroke, Duke of Lancaster, deposed his cousin King Richard II of England in 1399, establishing himself as Henry IV. Richard II had ruled since 1377 and was known for his autocratic style and unpopular policies. Henry's deposition was facilitated by the support of English nobles dissatisfied with Richard's rule. Richard was imprisoned and died in captivity in 1400 (or possibly 1402). Henry IV's accession marked the beginning of the House of Lancaster's rule and would eventually lead to the Wars of the Roses in the 15th century. The deposition of Richard II was a significant political event that demonstrated the power of the English nobility to remove an unpopular monarch.
key_figures: Henry Bolingbroke (later Henry IV of England)

sources.name: English royal records and parliamentary documents

events.certainty_note: The deposition of Richard II in 1399 is well-documented in contemporary English sources. The date and circumstances are firmly established.

events.title: Timur's Death (Central Asia)
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the Central Asian conqueror who had built a vast empire stretching from Anatolia to India, died in February 1405 (not 1399, but his health was declining in 1399). However, by 1399, Timur was in his late 70s and his empire was beginning to show signs of fragmentation. His sons and grandsons were already competing for power and territory. The year 1399 marks a period of uncertainty in the Timurid Empire as Timur's health declined and succession disputes began. After Timur's death in 1405, his empire fragmented into several successor states, including the Timurid Empire proper (centered in Persia and Central Asia) and various regional powers.
key_figures: Shah Rukh (son of Timur, later ruler of the Timurid Empire)
events.certainty_note: Timur died in February 1405, not 1399. However, 1399 marks a period of decline and succession disputes in the Timurid Empire. The exact dating of events in Timur's final years is somewhat uncertain.
cross_references: Leads to 1405-003 (Timur's death and empire fragmentation)

events.title: Ming Dynasty Consolidates Control (China)
events.description: The Ming Dynasty, which had overthrown the Mongol Yuan Dynasty in 1368, was consolidating its control over China in the late 14th century. By 1399, the Hongwu Emperor (r. 1368–1398) had recently died, and his son, the Jianwen Emperor (r. 1398–1402), was ruling. The Jianwen Emperor faced challenges from his uncle, the Prince of Yan (later the Yongle Emperor), who would eventually seize power in 1402. The year 1399 marks a period of internal political tension within the Ming Dynasty as the succession was contested. The Ming Dynasty was one of the most powerful and culturally significant dynasties in Chinese history.
key_figures: Zhu Di (Prince of Yan, later Yongle Emperor)
Hongwu Emperor (deceased father of Jianwen Emperor)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Ming Dynasty's consolidation of control and the succession disputes of 1399 are well-documented in the Ming Shilu and other contemporary sources.

events.title: Ottoman Empire Expands in the Balkans
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under the rule of Bayezid I (r. 1389–1402), was expanding its control over the Balkans in the late 14th and early 15th centuries. By 1399, the Ottomans had conquered much of the Balkans from the Byzantine Empire and various Balkan kingdoms. The Ottoman expansion in the Balkans was a gradual process of conquest and consolidation that would continue throughout the 15th century. The year 1399 marks a period of Ottoman military success and territorial expansion in the region.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans in the late 14th and early 15th centuries is well-documented in Ottoman and Byzantine sources.

events.title: Zheng He Born (Ming China)
events.description: Zheng He, the famous Ming Dynasty admiral and explorer, was born in 1371 (some sources say 1371, others 1372). By 1399, he was a young man in his late 20s, already serving in the Ming court as a eunuch and military officer. He would later become one of the most famous explorers in world history, commanding the massive treasure voyages across the Indian Ocean in the early 15th century. The year 1399 marks a point in Zheng He's early career before his rise to prominence.
key_figures: Zheng He (Ming Dynasty admiral and explorer)
Yongle Emperor (future patron of Zheng He)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's birth year is traditionally given as 1371, but some sources suggest 1372. The exact date is uncertain, but he was definitely born in the early 1370s.

disconfirming_evidence: The deposition of Richard II (1399-001) is sometimes presented as a straightforward act of noble rebellion, but modern scholarship emphasizes that it was a complex political event involving questions of legitimacy, parliamentary procedure, and the nature of kingship. The exact circumstances of Richard's deposition and death are debated by historians. The Ottoman expansion in the Balkans (1399-004) is sometimes presented as a simple military conquest, but it involved complex negotiations, alliances, and the absorption of existing Balkan kingdoms; it was not a straightforward process of conquest.
historiographic_note: The year 1399 is moderately well-documented in European, Islamic, and Chinese sources. European sources (English, Italian, Portuguese chronicles) are abundant. Islamic sources (Ottoman and Timurid chronicles) are available but often written decades after events. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are contemporary and detailed. However, sources from sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are sparse or absent. The surviving sources reflect the interests of literate elites and likely underrepresent the experiences of common people, women, and non-elite groups.

era_context: The year 1398 falls in the late 14th century, a period of significant political and cultural transition. In Europe, the late Medieval period was characterized by feudal fragmentation, dynastic conflicts, and the early stirrings of the Renaissance in Italy. In Asia, the Ming Dynasty was consolidating its rule in China, the Timurid Empire was at its height in Central Asia and Persia, and the Ottoman Empire was expanding in Anatolia and the Balkans. This is an era of cultural flourishing, military conflict, and dynastic transitions.

events.title: Hongwu Emperor Dies; Jianwen Emperor Ascends (Ming China)
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang), founder of the Ming Dynasty, died on June 24, 1398, at the age of 70. He had ruled for 30 years (1368–1398) and had established the Ming Dynasty as one of the most powerful and culturally significant dynasties in Chinese history. His son, the Jianwen Emperor (Zhu Yunwen), ascended to the throne at the age of 21. The Jianwen Emperor's reign would be marked by internal political conflict, particularly with his uncle, the Prince of Yan (later the Yongle Emperor), who would eventually seize power in 1402. The death of the Hongwu Emperor marked a significant transition in Ming Dynasty politics.
key_figures: Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang; Ming Dynasty founder, r. 1368–1398)
Jianwen Emperor (Zhu Yunwen; Ming Dynasty ruler, r. 1398–1402)
Zhu Di (Prince of Yan, later Yongle Emperor)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The death of the Hongwu Emperor on June 24, 1398, is well-documented in the Ming Shilu and other contemporary sources. The date is firmly established.

events.title: Timur's Last Campaign (Central Asia / Anatolia)
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the Central Asian conqueror, was in his late 70s in 1398 and was planning what would be his final major campaign—an invasion of China. He had already conquered vast territories stretching from Anatolia to India. In 1398, he was gathering his forces and making preparations for the invasion of China, which would begin in 1405 but would be cut short by his death in February 1405. The year 1398 marks the final phase of Timur's military career as he prepared for his last great conquest.
events.certainty_note: Timur's preparations for his final campaign in 1398 are documented in later Timurid chronicles. The exact chronology of his final years is somewhat uncertain.

events.title: Richard II Rules England (Late Medieval Period)
events.description: Richard II, King of England, was ruling in 1398 in the final year before his deposition. He had ruled since 1377 and was known for his autocratic style, artistic patronage, and unpopular policies. By 1398, Richard was facing increasing opposition from English nobles dissatisfied with his rule. His cousin, Henry Bolingbroke, Duke of Lancaster, was in exile and would return in 1399 to depose Richard. The year 1398 marks the final year of Richard II's reign before his dramatic fall from power.
key_figures: Henry Bolingbroke (Duke of Lancaster, later Henry IV)

sources.name: English royal records and parliamentary documents

events.certainty_note: Richard II's rule in 1398 is well-documented in contemporary English sources. The political situation leading to his deposition in 1399 is firmly established.

events.title: Ottoman Empire Under Bayezid I (Anatolia / Balkans)
events.description: Bayezid I (r. 1389–1402), known as 'Yildirim' (the Thunderbolt) for his military prowess, was ruling the Ottoman Empire in 1398. He had expanded Ottoman territory in Anatolia and the Balkans and was consolidating Ottoman power in the region. By 1398, the Ottoman Empire was one of the major powers in the eastern Mediterranean and was beginning to pose a serious threat to the Byzantine Empire. Bayezid I's reign marked a period of Ottoman military success and territorial expansion.
events.certainty_note: Bayezid I's rule of the Ottoman Empire in 1398 is well-documented in Ottoman and Byzantine sources.

Richard II's rule (1398-003) is sometimes presented as autocratic and unpopular, but modern scholarship emphasizes the complexity of his political situation and the role of factional conflicts among English nobles.

In Europe, the late Medieval period was characterized by feudal fragmentation, dynastic conflicts, and the early stirrings of the Renaissance in Italy. In Asia, the Ming Dynasty was consolidating its rule in China, the Timurid Empire was at its height in Central Asia and Persia, and the Ottoman Empire was expanding in Anatolia and the Balkans. events.title: Kalmar Union Established (Scandinavia)
events.description: The Kalmar Union was established in 1397 when the three Scandinavian kingdoms of Denmark, Norway, and Sweden were united under a single monarch, Margaret I of Denmark. The union was formalized at a meeting in Kalmar, Sweden, and Margaret I was recognized as the sovereign of all three kingdoms. The Kalmar Union lasted until 1523 and represented a significant political consolidation in Northern Europe. The union was designed to strengthen the Scandinavian kingdoms against the Hanseatic League and other external threats. Margaret I, known as 'the Lady King,' was one of the most powerful female rulers of medieval Europe.
key_figures: Margaret I of Denmark (Queen of Denmark, Norway, and Sweden)
Eric of Pomerania (Margaret's great-nephew, designated heir)

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on the Kalmar Union

events.certainty_note: The establishment of the Kalmar Union in 1397 is well-documented in Scandinavian sources. The date and circumstances are firmly established.
cross_references: Contemporary with other European dynastic consolidations

events.title: Hongwu Emperor Rules Ming China (Final Year)
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang), founder of the Ming Dynasty, was in the final year of his reign in 1397. He had ruled for 29 years (1368–1398) and had established the Ming Dynasty as one of the most powerful and culturally significant dynasties in Chinese history. The Hongwu Emperor was known for his authoritarian rule, his efforts to consolidate Ming power, and his cultural patronage. He would die in June 1398, and his son, the Jianwen Emperor, would ascend to the throne. The year 1397 marks the final phase of the Hongwu Emperor's reign.
key_figures: Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang; Ming Dynasty founder, r. 1368–1398)

By 1397, Timur was planning his final major campaign—an invasion of China—which would begin in 1405 but would be cut short by his death in February 1405. The year 1397 marks a period of consolidation and planning in the Timurid Empire as Timur prepared for his last great conquest.
events.certainty_note: Timur's consolidation of the Timurid Empire in 1397 is documented in later Timurid chronicles. The exact chronology of his final years is somewhat uncertain.

By 1397, the Ottoman Empire had become one of the major powers in the eastern Mediterranean and was posing an increasing threat to the Byzantine Empire. Bayezid I's reign marked a period of Ottoman military success and territorial expansion that would continue until his defeat by Timur in 1402.

disconfirming_evidence: The Kalmar Union (1397-001) is sometimes presented as a straightforward political consolidation, but modern scholarship emphasizes that it was a personal union under Margaret I and that the three kingdoms retained significant autonomy. The union was not as centralized as later nation-states, and tensions between the kingdoms would persist throughout the union's existence. The Hongwu Emperor's rule (1397-002) is sometimes presented as a period of stability, but his authoritarian policies and succession disputes suggest that the Ming Dynasty was facing internal challenges. Timur's consolidation of the Timurid Empire (1397-003) is sometimes presented as a straightforward process, but the empire was already showing signs of fragmentation as Timur's health declined.

Islamic sources (Ottoman and Timurid chronicles) are available but often written decades after events. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are contemporary and detailed. However, sources from sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are sparse or absent. The surviving sources reflect the interests of literate elites and likely underrepresent the experiences of common people, women, and non-elite groups. The historiography of 1397 is also shaped by the later significance of events that began in this year (e.g., the Kalmar Union, the final years of Richard II's reign), which may lead to retrospective overemphasis on their importance at the time.

era_context: The late 14th century witnessed the fragmentation of the Timurid Empire following Timur's death in 1405, though his successors still held considerable power across Central Asia and the Middle East. In Europe, the Ottoman Empire was consolidating control in the Balkans and Anatolia, while Western European kingdoms were engaged in dynastic conflicts—particularly the early stages of the Hundred Years' War between England and France. The Ming Dynasty in China (established 1368) was consolidating Han Chinese rule after the Yuan collapse. This was an era of significant military upheaval, religious reform movements, and early maritime exploration.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records from this period)

events.title: Battle of Nicopolis
events.description: A major Ottoman victory under Bayezid I against a Crusade army led by Hungarian King Sigismund and French nobility. The Ottoman forces decisively defeated the Christian coalition, killing thousands and capturing many nobles for ransom. This battle marked a turning point in Ottoman expansion into the Balkans and demonstrated the military superiority of Ottoman tactics. The defeat shattered Western European hopes of reversing Ottoman gains in southeastern Europe and effectively ended the last major Crusade effort of the medieval period.
key_figures: Sigismund of Hungary (King of Hungary and Holy Roman Emperor-elect)
Jean de Nevers (French nobleman, later Duke of Burgundy)

sources.name: Contemporary Ottoman chronicles and later European accounts (Froissart, Jean de Wavrin)
Ottoman administrative records and Turkish historiography

events.certainty_note: The battle date is well-established in both Ottoman and European sources. Contemporary accounts exist, though written shortly after the event rather than during it.
cross_references: Relates to Ottoman expansion trajectory documented in 1389 (Battle of Kosovo) and subsequent Balkan campaigns

events.title: Zheng He's early career in Ming service
events.description: Zheng He, a Muslim eunuch of Mongol descent, was rising in prominence within the Ming court under the Yongle Emperor's patronage. Though his famous voyages would not begin until 1405, his appointment to positions of military and administrative authority in the 1390s laid the groundwork for his later role as commander of the treasure fleets. This period reflects the Ming Dynasty's consolidation of power and its increasing interest in maritime projection and diplomatic outreach.
key_figures: Zheng He (Ming admiral and diplomat, then rising official)
Yongle Emperor (Ming Emperor, r. 1402–1424, but influential before formal reign)

sources.name: Ming Dynasty official records and later compilations (Ming Shilu)
Historiographic consensus on Zheng He's biography

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's exact career trajectory in the 1390s is less well-documented than his later voyages. The general timeline of his rise is accepted, but specific events in 1396 are inferred from broader career patterns.

events.title: Tamerlane (Timur) consolidates Central Asian power
events.description: Timur, the Turco-Mongol conqueror, was at the height of his power, having conquered vast territories from the Mediterranean to China. In 1396, he was organizing his empire and preparing for further campaigns. His court at Samarkand was becoming a center of Islamic culture and architecture. Though Timur would die in 1405, his dominance in this period shaped the geopolitical landscape of Central Asia, the Middle East, and parts of South Asia.
key_figures: Timur (Tamerlane, Timurid Empire founder and ruler)

sources.name: Contemporary Persian chronicles (Yazdi, Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi's Zafarnama)
Ottoman and Mamluk sources documenting Timurid relations

events.certainty_note: Timur's political dominance in 1396 is well-attested. The specific events of this year are less detailed, but his overall power and activities are historically secure.
cross_references: Part of broader Timurid expansion documented from 1370s onward

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Nicopolis is sometimes portrayed in Western sources as a straightforward 'Crusade' against the Ottomans, but modern scholarship emphasizes that it was primarily a Hungarian-led Balkan campaign with French and other European contingents, not a formally declared Crusade by the Papacy. The religious framing was partly retrospective. Additionally, some sources exaggerate the casualty figures and the degree of European unity in the campaign.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1396 is moderate but heavily weighted toward Ottoman-European military history. Ottoman and European chronicles provide detailed accounts of Nicopolis, but these sources have inherent biases—Ottoman sources emphasize Bayezid's military genius, while European sources often focus on the tragedy of Christian defeat. Chinese sources on Zheng He and Ming activities are more sparse for this specific year. Sub-Saharan African, Southeast Asian, and Pacific histories are largely absent from surviving written records for this year.

era_context: The late 14th century continued to be marked by major geopolitical shifts: the Ottoman Empire was expanding aggressively in the Balkans, the Timurid Empire dominated Central Asia and the Middle East, the Ming Dynasty was consolidating Han Chinese rule in East Asia, and Western European kingdoms were locked in dynastic conflicts. This was a period of significant military innovation, religious ferment (including early reform movements), and the early stages of European maritime exploration. The Hundred Years' War between England and France was in its mid-phase.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asian kingdoms (sparse documentation in European sources)
Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records in Old World sources)

events.title: Ottoman siege and conquest of Thessalonica
events.description: Ottoman forces under Bayezid I besieged the Byzantine city of Thessalonica, one of the major remaining Byzantine strongholds in the Balkans. The city fell to Ottoman control after a prolonged siege, though the exact date is disputed between 1395 and 1397 in different sources. The conquest marked a significant step in Ottoman consolidation of the Balkans and reduced Byzantine territorial holdings to little more than Constantinople itself. The fall of Thessalonica demonstrated Ottoman military capability and the declining power of the Byzantine Empire.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles and later Byzantine sources

events.certainty_note: The siege of Thessalonica is well-attested, but sources disagree on the exact year of conquest—some place it in 1395, others in 1397. The event is assigned to 1395 by convention, but ±2 years uncertainty is appropriate.
cross_references: Part of broader Ottoman Balkan expansion documented from 1389 onward

events.title: Timur's campaign in the Caucasus and southern Russia
events.description: Timur conducted military campaigns in the Caucasus region and southern Russia, consolidating Timurid control and extracting tribute from local rulers. These campaigns were part of Timur's broader strategy to maintain dominance over the vast territories he had conquered. The campaigns resulted in significant destruction and displacement of populations, though specific details of 1395 operations are less well-documented than some of his earlier or later campaigns.

sources.name: Persian chronicles (Yazdi, Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi's Zafarnama)
Russian and Caucasian sources documenting Timurid incursions

events.certainty_note: Timur's campaigns in the Caucasus and southern Russia are well-attested in general, but the specific events of 1395 are less precisely documented than some other years. The broader pattern of his military activity is secure.
cross_references: Part of Timurid expansion documented from 1370s onward

events.title: John of Gaunt's death and impact on English succession
events.description: John of Gaunt, Duke of Lancaster and one of the most powerful English nobles, died in February 1395. His death had significant implications for English politics and the succession to the throne. King Richard II's seizure of Gaunt's estates (contrary to the rights of Gaunt's son Henry Bolingbroke) contributed to the political tensions that would lead to Richard's deposition in 1399. Gaunt's death marked the end of an era of powerful magnate influence and set the stage for the dynastic conflicts of the 15th century.
key_figures: John of Gaunt (Duke of Lancaster, died 1395)
Henry Bolingbroke (future Henry IV, son of John of Gaunt)
events.certainty_note: John of Gaunt's death date is well-documented in English records. The political significance is clear from contemporary and near-contemporary sources.
cross_references: Led directly to 1399-001 (Deposition of Richard II)

events.title: Council of Constance preparations and religious reform movements
events.description: In the mid-1390s, discussions and preparations were underway for what would become the Council of Constance (1414–1418), though the council itself was not convened until later. The period saw growing calls for church reform, particularly in response to the Western Schism (1378–1417), which had divided papal authority. Reformers like Jan Hus in Bohemia and John Wycliffe's followers in England were gaining influence. These movements reflected deep dissatisfaction with ecclesiastical corruption and would shape religious history for the next century.
key_figures: John Wycliffe (English reformer, died 1384 but influence continuing)
Various church councils and bishops discussing reform

sources.name: Church records and theological writings from the period
Historiographic consensus on pre-Reformation reform movements

events.certainty_note: The broader context of reform movements in the 1390s is well-established. Specific events in 1395 are less precisely dated but fit within the documented trajectory of religious ferment.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Thessalonica's fall is disputed in sources. While often assigned to 1395, some Ottoman and Byzantine sources place it in 1397. Modern scholarship has not reached complete consensus, and the siege may have lasted from 1395 to 1397. Additionally, the characterization of John of Gaunt's death as a simple 'political event' obscures the complex factional politics of Richard II's court—some sources suggest Richard's actions were not simply tyrannical but reflected genuine concerns about magnate power.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1395 is moderate, with strong coverage of Ottoman-Balkan military history and English political history. Ottoman and English sources are relatively abundant, though they reflect the biases of their respective courts. Chinese, Persian, and Central Asian sources are less well-integrated into Western historiography. Sub-Saharan African, Southeast Asian, and Pacific histories remain largely absent from surviving written records for this year. The religious reform movements are documented primarily through later compilations and theological texts rather than contemporary records of specific 1395 events.

John of Gaunt's death and Richard II's seizure of his estates directly precipitated the political crisis leading to Richard's deposition
Religious reform movements of the 1390s directly led to the convening of the Council of Constance in 1414

era_context: The late 14th century was characterized by major geopolitical upheaval: the Ottoman Empire was aggressively expanding in the Balkans, the Timurid Empire dominated Central Asia and the Middle East, the Ming Dynasty was consolidating Han Chinese rule after the Yuan collapse, and Western European kingdoms were engaged in dynastic conflicts. The Western Schism in the Catholic Church (1378–1417) was creating religious and political instability. This was an era of significant military innovation, early maritime exploration, and the emergence of early modern state structures.

events.title: Ottoman consolidation in Anatolia and the Balkans
events.description: Bayezid I continued consolidating Ottoman power in Anatolia and the Balkans, eliminating rival Turkish beyliks and extending Ottoman control over former Byzantine and Serbian territories. This period saw the Ottoman Empire transition from a regional power to a major Mediterranean and Balkan force. The consolidation involved both military campaigns and diplomatic maneuvering, setting the stage for the major Ottoman expansion of the late 1390s and early 1400s.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman consolidation in this period is well-attested in general, but specific events of 1394 are less precisely documented than some other years. The broader pattern is secure.
cross_references: Part of Ottoman expansion documented from 1389 onward

events.title: Ming Dynasty's consolidation of Han Chinese rule
events.description: The Ming Dynasty, established in 1368 after the collapse of the Yuan Dynasty, continued consolidating Han Chinese rule over China. In 1394, the Yongle Emperor (though not yet formally on the throne—he would seize power in 1402) was rising in prominence. The period saw the establishment of Ming administrative structures, the suppression of remaining Yuan loyalists, and the beginning of major construction projects including work on the Forbidden City. This consolidation laid the groundwork for the Ming Dynasty's later maritime expansion and cultural flowering.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (future Ming emperor, then Prince of Yan)
events.certainty_note: Ming consolidation in the 1390s is well-attested in general. Specific events of 1394 are less precisely documented but fit within the broader pattern of administrative consolidation.
cross_references: Part of Ming Dynasty consolidation documented from 1368 onward

events.title: Expulsion of Jews from France
events.description: King Charles VI of France issued an edict expelling Jews from France in 1394, continuing a pattern of religious persecution and economic exploitation that had characterized medieval European policy toward Jewish communities. This expulsion was part of broader anti-Jewish sentiment in late medieval Europe and reflected the religious intolerance of the period. The expulsion had significant demographic and economic consequences for French Jewish communities and contributed to the migration of Jews to other regions, particularly the Mediterranean and Eastern Europe.

sources.name: Jewish historical sources and later historiography

events.certainty_note: The expulsion of Jews from France in 1394 is well-documented in both French royal records and Jewish historical sources. The date is secure.
cross_references: Part of broader pattern of medieval European anti-Jewish persecution

events.title: Timur's consolidation of Central Asian power
events.description: Timur continued consolidating his vast empire, which stretched from the Mediterranean to China. In 1394, he was organizing administrative structures and preparing for further campaigns. His court at Samarkand was becoming a major center of Islamic culture, architecture, and learning. Timur's dominance shaped the geopolitical landscape of Central Asia, the Middle East, and parts of South Asia and would continue to do so until his death in 1405.
key_figures: Timur (Tamerlane, Timurid Empire founder and ruler)

sources.name: Persian chronicles (Yazdi, Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi's Zafarnama)

events.certainty_note: Timur's political dominance in 1394 is well-attested. Specific events of this year are less precisely documented than some other years.
cross_references: Part of Timurid expansion documented from 1370s onward

disconfirming_evidence: The characterization of the 1394 expulsion of Jews from France as a simple 'legal' event obscures the complex economic and political motivations behind it. Some historians argue that the expulsion was primarily motivated by Charles VI's desire to seize Jewish property and settle debts, rather than purely religious intolerance. Additionally, the expulsion was not uniformly enforced across all French territories, and some Jewish communities were able to negotiate exemptions or return within a few years.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1394 is moderate, with relatively strong coverage of Ottoman, Ming, and Western European political history. Ottoman and French sources are abundant, though they reflect court biases. Chinese sources on Ming consolidation are less well-integrated into Western historiography. Persian and Central Asian sources on Timur are available but often reflect the perspective of the conqueror. Sub-Saharan African, Southeast Asian, and Pacific histories remain largely absent from surviving written records for this year.

events.title: Ottoman campaigns in the Balkans under Bayezid I
events.description: Bayezid I continued Ottoman military campaigns in the Balkans, consolidating Ottoman control over former Byzantine and Serbian territories. These campaigns were part of the broader Ottoman expansion that would culminate in the Battle of Nicopolis in 1396. The campaigns involved both direct military conquest and diplomatic pressure on local rulers to accept Ottoman suzerainty. The period saw the Ottoman Empire transition from a regional Anatolian power to a major Balkan force.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman military activity in the Balkans during this period is well-attested in general. Specific events of 1393 are less precisely documented than some other years.

events.title: Hongwu Emperor's continued consolidation of Ming rule
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor, founder of the Ming Dynasty, continued consolidating Han Chinese rule over China in 1393. The period saw the establishment of Ming administrative structures, the suppression of remaining Yuan loyalists, and the beginning of major construction projects. The Hongwu Emperor was known for his authoritarian rule and his efforts to centralize power. His reign laid the groundwork for the Ming Dynasty's later cultural and maritime achievements.
events.certainty_note: Ming consolidation under the Hongwu Emperor in the 1390s is well-attested in general. Specific events of 1393 are less precisely documented.
cross_references: Part of Ming Dynasty consolidation documented from 1368 onward

His dominance stretched from the Mediterranean to China, and he was organizing administrative structures to maintain control over these territories. Timur's court at Samarkand was becoming a major center of Islamic culture and learning. His military campaigns and administrative innovations shaped the geopolitical landscape of Central Asia, the Middle East, and parts of South Asia.

events.title: Richard II's consolidation of power in England
events.description: King Richard II of England was consolidating his power in 1393, following his earlier conflicts with the nobility. The period saw Richard attempting to establish a more centralized and authoritarian form of rule, which would eventually lead to conflicts with the English magnates. Richard's policies and personality would contribute to the political tensions that would culminate in his deposition in 1399. The period reflects the broader late medieval trend toward stronger centralized monarchy, though Richard's approach was more confrontational than that of his contemporaries.
events.certainty_note: Richard II's political activities in 1393 are documented in English sources, though specific events of this year are less precisely dated than some other years.

Some historians argue that Richard's actions were defensive responses to magnate power rather than aggressive attempts at tyranny. Additionally, the sources on Richard II are heavily influenced by the perspective of his opponents, who later justified his deposition through hostile chronicles.

events.title: Establishment of the Joseon Dynasty in Korea
events.description: The Joseon Dynasty was established in Korea in 1392, following the collapse of the Goryeo Dynasty. General Yi Seong-gye founded the new dynasty, which would rule Korea for over 500 years until 1910. The establishment of Joseon marked a significant shift in Korean politics and culture, with the new dynasty adopting Neo-Confucianism as its state ideology and establishing a more centralized administrative system. The founding of Joseon had important implications for East Asian geopolitics, as it created a new major power in the region and shifted the balance of power in relation to the Ming Dynasty in China.
key_figures: Yi Seong-gye (Taejo, founder of Joseon Dynasty, r. 1392–1398)

sources.name: Joseon Dynasty official records (Joseon Wangjo Sillok)
Chinese Ming Dynasty records documenting Korean relations

events.certainty_note: The founding of the Joseon Dynasty in 1392 is well-documented in both Korean and Chinese sources. The date is secure.
cross_references: Part of broader East Asian geopolitical shifts in the late 14th century

The consolidation involved both military campaigns and diplomatic maneuvering, setting the stage for the major Ottoman expansion of the late 1390s.

The Hongwu Emperor was in the later years of his reign, and the period saw the establishment of Ming administrative structures, the suppression of remaining Yuan loyalists, and the beginning of major construction projects. The consolidation laid the groundwork for the Ming Dynasty's later maritime expansion and cultural flowering.
events.certainty_note: Ming consolidation in the 1390s is well-attested in general. Specific events of 1392 are less precisely documented but fit within the broader pattern of administrative consolidation.

disconfirming_evidence: The founding of the Joseon Dynasty is sometimes portrayed as a straightforward 'revolution' against the Goryeo Dynasty, but modern scholarship emphasizes that it was a gradual transition with significant continuity in administrative structures and personnel. Additionally, Yi Seong-gye's motivations were complex, involving both genuine reform impulses and personal political ambition. The characterization of the founding as purely ideological (Neo-Confucianism vs. Buddhism) obscures the political and military factors that were equally important.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1392 is moderate, with relatively strong coverage of East Asian, Ottoman, and Ming political history. Korean sources on the Joseon founding are abundant and detailed. Ottoman and Chinese sources are also available, though they reflect court biases. Persian and Central Asian sources on Timur are available but often reflect the perspective of the conqueror. Sub-Saharan African, Southeast Asian, and Pacific histories remain largely absent from surviving written records for this year. The founding of Joseon is one of the most significant events of 1392 and represents an important shift in East Asian geopolitics that is sometimes underrepresented in Western-centric historical narratives.

era_context: The late 14th century saw the consolidation of major Eurasian powers: the Ming Dynasty in China (established 1368) was expanding its administrative reach; the Ottoman Empire was in rapid territorial expansion in Anatolia and the Balkans; Western Europe was fragmented among competing kingdoms and principalities during the late medieval period; and the Timurid Empire controlled Central Asia and Persia following Timur's conquests. This was an era of significant cultural flowering in Islamic lands, early Renaissance stirrings in Italy, and technological advancement in navigation and printing.

Southeast Asia (sparse European documentation; local chronicles less accessible)

events.title: Pogroms against Jews in Castile and Aragon (Valencian Massacres)
events.region: Iberian Peninsula (Kingdom of Castile, Kingdom of Aragon, Valencia)
events.description: Severe anti-Jewish violence erupted across the Crown of Aragon and Castile, particularly in Valencia, Barcelona, and Seville. Mobs, often incited by clerical preaching and economic resentment, attacked Jewish quarters (aljamas), killing hundreds and forcing mass conversions. The violence was partly triggered by a sermon by Ferran Martínez, an archdeacon of Écija, who had been preaching against Jews for years. These pogroms marked a turning point in Iberian Jewish history, leading to the forced conversion of thousands and the beginning of the end for medieval Spanish Jewry.
key_figures: King John I of Aragon (attempted to restore order)

sources.name: Contemporary Jewish chronicles and responsa (Shevet Yehudah by Solomon ibn Verga, compiled later but based on contemporary accounts)
Aragonese royal documents and municipal records

events.certainty_note: The pogroms are well-documented in both Jewish and Christian sources from the period. The exact death toll varies by source (estimates range from hundreds to thousands), but the event's occurrence and significance are not disputed.
cross_references: Follows from anti-Jewish sentiment building through the 1380s
Led to the Spanish Inquisition's establishment in 1478 and eventual expulsion of 1492

events.title: Ottoman conquest of Aydın and consolidation in western Anatolia
events.description: The Ottoman forces under Murad I continued their systematic conquest of the remaining Turkish beyliks (principalities) in western Anatolia. The capture of Aydın and surrounding territories consolidated Ottoman control over the Aegean coast and eliminated rival Turkish states. This expansion was part of Murad I's broader strategy to unify Anatolia under Ottoman rule and prepare for campaigns into the Balkans. The conquest strengthened Ottoman naval capabilities and economic resources.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles (Aşıkpaşazade's History of the House of Osman, compiled in 15th century)

events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in this period is well-attested, though exact dating of individual conquests varies by source. The consolidation of western Anatolia occurred over several years; 1391 is approximate for Aydın's fall.
cross_references: Led to Ottoman dominance in Anatolia by early 15th century

events.title: Ming Dynasty's Yongle Emperor begins consolidation of power
events.description: The Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di), who would formally ascend to the throne in 1402 after the Jingnan Rebellion, was consolidating his power base as Prince of Yan in northern China. His military and administrative capabilities were becoming increasingly evident, and he was building alliances among military commanders and officials. This period laid the groundwork for his eventual usurpation of the throne from his nephew and his subsequent transformation of the Ming Dynasty into a centralized, expansionist power.
key_figures: Zhu Di, Prince of Yan (future Yongle Emperor)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Official Ming histories compiled by later dynasties

events.certainty_note: The Yongle Emperor's rise to power is well-documented, though the exact timeline of his consolidation of power in 1391 is inferred from broader historical narratives rather than specific dated events.
cross_references: Resulted in Yongle's reign (1402–1424) and major Ming expansion

events.title: Timur's court in Samarkand at height of cultural patronage
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the great Central Asian conqueror, was in his final years (he died in 1405) and had established Samarkand as one of the world's great cultural centers. He patronized architects, poets, scholars, and craftsmen, commissioning magnificent buildings and supporting intellectual pursuits. The Timurid court was a nexus of Persian, Islamic, and Central Asian culture, attracting talent from across Eurasia. This period saw the flourishing of Persian literature, miniature painting, and architectural innovation that would define the Timurid Renaissance.
key_figures: Ulugh Beg (Timur's grandson, future astronomer and patron)

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi's Zafarnama (Book of Victories), commissioned by Timur's son
Timurid court records and architectural evidence
Contemporary accounts by travelers (Ibn Battuta's earlier travels, Clavijo's embassy in 1403–1406)

events.certainty_note: Timur's patronage of Samarkand and the cultural flowering of the Timurid court are well-documented through both textual sources and surviving architectural evidence.
cross_references: Part of broader Timurid cultural renaissance (1370–1500s)
Influenced later Islamic and Persian culture

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the 1391 Iberian pogroms has been debated by historians regarding their immediate causes: some scholars emphasize economic competition and social tensions, while others stress religious ideology and clerical incitement. The relative weight of these factors remains contested. Additionally, the dating of some Ottoman conquests in Anatolia is approximate; some sources place the fall of Aydın slightly earlier or later. The Yongle Emperor's role in 1391 is inferred from broader political narratives rather than specific documented actions in that year.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1391 is uneven. Western European and Islamic sources are relatively rich, particularly for the Iberian Peninsula and Ottoman Empire. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are detailed but often focus on court politics rather than broader social history. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are largely absent from surviving written records for this year. The sources that do exist are often written from elite perspectives (court chronicles, religious institutions) and may underrepresent popular movements, economic conditions, and the experiences of non-elite populations. Jewish sources for the 1391 pogroms were often compiled later, though they preserve earlier oral traditions and written records.

era_context: The late 14th century remained a period of significant political fragmentation and consolidation across Eurasia. The Ming Dynasty was firmly established in China; the Ottoman Empire was expanding aggressively; Western Europe was divided among competing kingdoms with the Hundred Years' War between England and France ongoing; and the Timurid Empire dominated Central Asia and Persia. This was a transitional period between medieval and early modern political structures.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Oceania and Pacific islands (minimal contemporary records)

events.title: Battle of Bileća and Ottoman expansion in the Balkans
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murad I engaged in campaigns to consolidate control over the Balkans, including operations in Bosnia and Herzegovina. The Ottoman expansion into the Balkans was methodical and relentless, gradually reducing the independence of Serbian, Bulgarian, and Bosnian states. These campaigns were part of Murad I's strategy to establish Ottoman hegemony in southeastern Europe and prepare the ground for later major offensives. The conquest of Balkan territories provided the Ottomans with resources, manpower, and strategic positions for further expansion.
key_figures: Tvrtko I (Bosnian King, attempting to resist Ottoman expansion)
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans during this period is well-attested, though specific battles and their exact dates are sometimes uncertain. The Battle of Bileća is documented in later sources but with varying dates.
cross_references: Part of broader Ottoman Balkan expansion (1360–1400)
Led to Ottoman dominance in the Balkans by early 15th century

events.title: Zheng He's early career in Ming military service
events.description: Zheng He, the famous Ming admiral and explorer, was serving in the military forces of the Prince of Yan (future Yongle Emperor) in northern China. At this time, Zheng He was building his reputation as a capable military commander and administrator. His service to the Prince of Yan during this period would lead to his later prominence as the commander of the great treasure voyages (1405–1433) after the Yongle Emperor ascended to the throne. Zheng He's early career demonstrates the meritocratic aspects of Ming military organization.
key_figures: Zheng He (Ming admiral and military commander)
Zhu Di, Prince of Yan (future Yongle Emperor)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's service to the Prince of Yan is documented, though specific events in 1390 are not individually recorded. His career trajectory is inferred from broader historical narratives.
cross_references: Led to Zheng He's command of treasure voyages (1405–1433)
Part of broader Ming military reorganization under Yongle

events.title: Chaucer completes or near-completes the Canterbury Tales
events.description: Geoffrey Chaucer, the English poet, was in the final years of his life (he died in 1400) and had completed or was completing his masterwork, the Canterbury Tales. This vernacular literary work, written in Middle English, represented a significant shift toward the use of English rather than French or Latin for high literature. The Canterbury Tales depicted a diverse cross-section of English society and became one of the foundational works of English literature. Chaucer's work reflected the growing cultural confidence of the English language and the emergence of a distinct English literary tradition.

sources.name: Surviving manuscripts of the Canterbury Tales

events.certainty_note: The exact date of completion of the Canterbury Tales is uncertain; scholars generally place it between 1387 and 1400. The work was likely still being revised in 1390.
cross_references: Part of broader emergence of vernacular literature in late medieval Europe
Influenced English literary tradition for centuries

events.title: Gian Galeazzo Visconti consolidates power in Milan
events.description: Gian Galeazzo Visconti, the Duke of Milan, was consolidating his power and expanding Milanese territory through a combination of military conquest and diplomatic maneuvering. He was one of the most powerful rulers in Italy and was engaged in conflicts with other Italian states, particularly Florence and Venice. Visconti's Milan was a center of cultural patronage and early Renaissance development. His political and military strategies made Milan one of the dominant powers in northern Italy during the late 14th century.
events.certainty_note: Gian Galeazzo Visconti's consolidation of power in Milan is well-documented in contemporary records and later chronicles.
cross_references: Part of broader Italian political fragmentation and consolidation
Led to Milan's prominence in early Renaissance Italy

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the Battle of Bileća is disputed among historians; some sources place it in 1388, others in 1390 or later. The significance of Chaucer's completion of the Canterbury Tales in 1390 specifically is uncertain; the work was likely still being revised, and the exact date of completion remains unknown. Some scholars argue that Chaucer continued revising the work until his death in 1400.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1390 is moderate but uneven. Western European sources (English, Italian, and Ottoman chronicles) are relatively abundant. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are detailed for court politics but less so for broader social history. Islamic sources for the Ottoman Empire are available but often compiled later. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are largely absent from surviving written records. The sources that do exist are often written from elite perspectives and may underrepresent popular movements and non-elite experiences.

era_context: The late 14th century was marked by the consolidation of major Eurasian powers and the beginning of significant geopolitical shifts. The Ming Dynasty was firmly established in China; the Ottoman Empire was in rapid expansion; Western Europe was fragmented among competing kingdoms; and the Timurid Empire dominated Central Asia. This period saw the transition from medieval to early modern political structures and the emergence of new technologies and cultural forms.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Oceania and Pacific islands (minimal contemporary records)

events.title: Battle of Kosovo (First Battle of Kosovo)
events.description: The Battle of Kosovo, fought on June 28, 1389 (June 15 in the Julian calendar), was a major military engagement between the Ottoman Empire under Murad I and a coalition of Balkan Christian states led by Serbian Prince Lazar Hrebeljanović. The battle resulted in heavy casualties on both sides and is traditionally portrayed as an Ottoman victory, though the outcome was tactically ambiguous. Murad I was killed in the battle, as was Prince Lazar. The battle became a defining moment in Serbian national mythology and marked a turning point in Ottoman expansion into the Balkans. The aftermath of Kosovo led to Ottoman suzerainty over Serbian and other Balkan territories, though full conquest took decades.
key_figures: Murad I (Ottoman Sultan, killed in battle)
Lazar Hrebeljanović (Serbian Prince, killed in battle)

sources.name: Serbian chronicles and oral traditions (Memorijal, later compilations)
Contemporary Venetian and Genoese merchant records

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Kosovo is one of the most significant events of the late 14th century and is well-documented in multiple sources, though details vary. The date (June 28, 1389) is confirmed by contemporary sources. The tactical outcome and significance have been debated by historians.
cross_references: Led to Ottoman succession under Bayezid I
Marked the beginning of Ottoman dominance in the Balkans
Became a central event in Serbian national identity

events.title: Bayezid I becomes Ottoman Sultan
events.description: Following the death of Murad I at the Battle of Kosovo, his son Bayezid I ascended to the Ottoman throne. Bayezid I, known as 'Yıldırım' (the Thunderbolt) for his military prowess, would become one of the most successful Ottoman sultans. He immediately consolidated his power, eliminating potential rivals and continuing the Ottoman expansion in Anatolia and the Balkans. Bayezid I's reign (1389–1402) was marked by aggressive military campaigns and the expansion of Ottoman territory to its greatest extent before the Timurid invasion of 1402.
events.certainty_note: Bayezid I's succession to the Ottoman throne in 1389 is well-documented in multiple sources.
cross_references: Led to Ottoman expansion under Bayezid I (1389–1402)

events.title: Hongwu Emperor's reign continues in Ming China
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang), founder of the Ming Dynasty, was in the final years of his reign (he died in 1398). He continued to consolidate Ming power and expand the dynasty's administrative reach. The Hongwu Emperor was known for his authoritarian rule, his purges of officials he suspected of disloyalty, and his efforts to restore Han Chinese cultural dominance after the Mongol Yuan Dynasty. His reign saw significant military campaigns against remaining Mongol forces in the north and the consolidation of Ming control over China.
key_figures: Zhu Yunwen (designated heir, future Jianwen Emperor)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's reign and his activities in 1389 are well-documented in Ming records.
cross_references: Led to Hongwu Emperor's death in 1398

events.title: Timur's campaigns in the Caucasus and Persia
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the great Central Asian conqueror, was conducting military campaigns in the Caucasus and Persia to consolidate his empire and suppress rebellions. Timur's campaigns were characterized by their brutality and effectiveness; he used psychological warfare, mass destruction, and rapid movement to overwhelm his enemies. These campaigns were part of Timur's broader strategy to create a unified empire under his control and to extract tribute from conquered territories. The campaigns in the Caucasus and Persia were particularly significant for establishing Timurid control over key trade routes and resources.

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi's Zafarnama (Book of Victories)
Contemporary accounts by travelers and chroniclers

events.certainty_note: Timur's campaigns in the Caucasus and Persia during this period are documented in later sources, though specific dates and details vary.
cross_references: Led to consolidation of Timurid Empire

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Kosovo's tactical outcome and significance have been debated by modern historians. While traditionally portrayed as an Ottoman victory, some scholars argue that the battle was tactically inconclusive or even a Serbian victory in the short term. The long-term Ottoman dominance in the Balkans was not immediately established after Kosovo; Serbian independence continued for several more decades. The mythologization of the battle in Serbian national identity has sometimes obscured the historical reality. Additionally, the exact composition of the Serbian coalition at Kosovo is disputed; some sources list different participants.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1389 is moderate to good, particularly for the Battle of Kosovo, which is one of the most significant events of the late 14th century. However, the sources for Kosovo are often written from partisan perspectives (Ottoman, Serbian, Byzantine) and were often compiled decades or centuries after the event. The battle's significance in Serbian national mythology has led to some romanticization and mythologization of the event. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are detailed for court politics. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are largely absent from surviving written records. The sources that do exist are often written from elite perspectives and may underrepresent popular movements and non-elite experiences.

era_context: The late 14th century was a period of significant political consolidation and transformation across Eurasia. The Ming Dynasty was firmly established in China; the Ottoman Empire was in rapid expansion in Anatolia and the Balkans; Western Europe was fragmented among competing kingdoms with the Hundred Years' War ongoing; and the Timurid Empire dominated Central Asia and Persia. This was a transitional period between medieval and early modern political structures.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Oceania and Pacific islands (minimal contemporary records)

events.title: Hongwu Emperor's purges of officials in Ming China
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor continued his systematic purges of officials he suspected of disloyalty or corruption. These purges, which had been ongoing since the early years of his reign, were particularly intense in the late 1380s. The Hongwu Emperor was known for his paranoia and his willingness to execute high-ranking officials on suspicion of treason. These purges had significant effects on Ming governance, creating an atmosphere of fear and uncertainty among the bureaucracy. The purges also contributed to the centralization of power in the hands of the emperor and the weakening of institutional checks on imperial authority.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's purges are well-documented in Ming records, though the exact number of victims and the specific dates of individual purges vary by source.
cross_references: Part of broader Hongwu Emperor's consolidation of power (1368–1398)
Led to centralization of Ming authority

events.title: Ottoman campaigns in Anatolia and the Balkans
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Murad I continued its systematic conquest of remaining Turkish beyliks in Anatolia and expansion into the Balkans. These campaigns were part of Murad I's strategy to unify Anatolia under Ottoman rule and establish Ottoman hegemony in southeastern Europe. The Ottoman military machine was highly effective, using a combination of cavalry tactics, siege warfare, and administrative consolidation to expand and consolidate Ottoman territory. These campaigns laid the groundwork for the Ottoman Empire's emergence as a major regional power.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in this period is well-attested, though specific campaigns and their exact dates vary by source.
cross_references: Led to Ottoman dominance in Anatolia and the Balkans by early 15th century

events.title: Gian Galeazzo Visconti expands Milanese territory
events.description: Gian Galeazzo Visconti, the Duke of Milan, continued his aggressive expansion of Milanese territory through military conquest and diplomatic maneuvering. In 1388, Visconti was engaged in conflicts with neighboring Italian states and was consolidating his control over Lombardy. His military campaigns and political strategies made Milan one of the dominant powers in northern Italy. Visconti's Milan was also a center of cultural patronage and early Renaissance development, attracting artists, scholars, and craftsmen.
events.certainty_note: Gian Galeazzo Visconti's expansion of Milanese territory is well-documented in contemporary records.
cross_references: Part of broader Italian political consolidation
Led to Milan's prominence in early Renaissance Italy

events.title: Timur's court in Samarkand continues cultural patronage
events.description: Timur, the great Central Asian conqueror, continued his patronage of culture and architecture in Samarkand. He commissioned magnificent buildings, supported poets and scholars, and made Samarkand one of the world's great cultural centers. The Timurid court was a nexus of Persian, Islamic, and Central Asian culture, attracting talent from across Eurasia. This period saw the flourishing of Persian literature, miniature painting, and architectural innovation that would define the Timurid Renaissance.

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi's Zafarnama (Book of Victories)
Timurid court records and architectural evidence

events.certainty_note: Timur's patronage of Samarkand and the cultural flowering of the Timurid court are well-documented through textual sources and surviving architectural evidence.
cross_references: Part of broader Timurid cultural renaissance (1370–1500s)
Influenced later Islamic and Persian culture

disconfirming_evidence: The exact number of victims of the Hongwu Emperor's purges is disputed; some sources give much higher numbers than others. The significance of individual Ottoman campaigns in 1388 is sometimes unclear; some sources conflate campaigns from different years. The dating of some of Gian Galeazzo Visconti's military campaigns is approximate.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1388 is moderate. Western European and Islamic sources are relatively abundant, particularly for the Ottoman Empire and Italian states. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are detailed for court politics. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are largely absent from surviving written records. The sources that do exist are often written from elite perspectives (court chronicles, state records) and may underrepresent popular movements, economic conditions, and the experiences of non-elite populations.

The Ming Dynasty was firmly established in China under the Hongwu Emperor; the Ottoman Empire was in rapid expansion in Anatolia and the Balkans; Western Europe was fragmented among competing kingdoms with the Hundred Years' War ongoing; and the Timurid Empire dominated Central Asia and Persia. This was a transitional period between medieval and early modern political structures, marked by the emergence of new technologies, cultural forms, and geopolitical alignments.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Oceania and Pacific islands (minimal contemporary records)

events.title: Hongwu Emperor's military campaigns against Mongol remnants
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor continued his military campaigns against remaining Mongol forces in the north and northwest of China. These campaigns were part of the Hongwu Emperor's broader strategy to consolidate Ming control over all of China and to eliminate the threat posed by Mongol remnants. The campaigns were generally successful, pushing Mongol forces further north and establishing Ming military dominance in northern China. These military operations also served to strengthen the Ming military apparatus and to consolidate the Hongwu Emperor's control over the military establishment.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's military campaigns against Mongol forces are well-documented in Ming records.
cross_references: Part of broader Hongwu Emperor's consolidation of Ming power (1368–1398)
Led to Ming military dominance in northern China

events.title: Ottoman expansion in Anatolia and the Balkans continues
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Murad I continued its systematic conquest of remaining Turkish beyliks in Anatolia and expansion into the Balkans. The Ottoman military machine was highly effective, using a combination of cavalry tactics, siege warfare, and administrative consolidation to expand and consolidate Ottoman territory. These campaigns were part of Murad I's strategy to unify Anatolia under Ottoman rule and establish Ottoman hegemony in southeastern Europe. The Ottoman expansion in 1387 laid the groundwork for the major military confrontation at the Battle of Kosovo in 1389.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in this period is well-attested, though specific campaigns and their exact dates vary by source.
cross_references: Led to the Battle of Kosovo in 1389

events.title: Geoffrey Chaucer's Canterbury Tales in progress
events.description: Geoffrey Chaucer, the English poet, was actively working on the Canterbury Tales, his masterwork. The Canterbury Tales, written in Middle English, represented a significant shift toward the use of English rather than French or Latin for high literature. The work depicted a diverse cross-section of English society and became one of the foundational works of English literature. Chaucer's work reflected the growing cultural confidence of the English language and the emergence of a distinct English literary tradition. The Canterbury Tales would be completed by 1400 and would have a profound influence on English literature.

sources.name: Surviving manuscripts of the Canterbury Tales

events.certainty_note: Chaucer was actively working on the Canterbury Tales in the late 1380s; the exact date of composition for individual tales is uncertain.
cross_references: Part of broader emergence of vernacular literature in late medieval Europe
Influenced English literary tradition for centuries

events.title: Timur's campaigns in Mesopotamia and Persia
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the great Central Asian conqueror, was conducting military campaigns in Mesopotamia and Persia to consolidate his empire and suppress rebellions. Timur's campaigns were characterized by their brutality and effectiveness; he used psychological warfare, mass destruction, and rapid movement to overwhelm his enemies. These campaigns were part of Timur's broader strategy to create a unified empire under his control and to extract tribute from conquered territories. The campaigns in Mesopotamia and Persia were particularly significant for establishing Timurid control over key trade routes and resources.

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi's Zafarnama (Book of Victories)
Contemporary accounts by travelers and chroniclers

events.certainty_note: Timur's campaigns in Mesopotamia and Persia during this period are documented in later sources, though specific dates and details vary.
cross_references: Led to consolidation of Timurid Empire

The significance of individual Ottoman campaigns in 1387 is sometimes unclear. The exact date of Chaucer's work on the Canterbury Tales is uncertain; scholars generally place the composition between 1387 and 1400, but the specific timeline is debated.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1387 is moderate. Western European and Islamic sources are relatively abundant, particularly for the Ottoman Empire and English literature. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are detailed for court politics and military campaigns. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are largely absent from surviving written records. The sources that do exist are often written from elite perspectives (court chronicles, state records, literary works) and may underrepresent popular movements, economic conditions, and the experiences of non-elite populations. The Canterbury Tales, while a primary source for English society, is a literary work and should not be read as a straightforward historical document.

China under the Ming Dynasty (established 1368) is in its early consolidation phase under the Hongwu Emperor. The Hundred Years' War between England and France continues with shifting fortunes. The Iberian Peninsula remains fragmented among Christian kingdoms and the Nasrid Emirate of Granada.

events.title: Battle of Pločnik
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murat I defeated Serbian forces at Pločnik, marking a significant Ottoman advance into the Balkans. This victory consolidated Ottoman control over central Serbia and demonstrated the military superiority of Ottoman cavalry tactics over feudal Serbian forces. The battle was part of the broader Ottoman conquest of the Balkans that would culminate in the Battle of Kosovo in 1389.

sources.name: Serbian historical tradition and historiographic consensus

events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in Ottoman and Serbian sources, but contemporary documentation is limited. Dating to 1386 is accepted by modern scholarship, though some sources place it in 1385 or 1387.
cross_references: 1389-001 (Battle of Kosovo, continuation of Ottoman expansion)

events.title: Marriage of John of Gaunt and Katherine Swynford
events.description: John of Gaunt, Duke of Lancaster and son of Edward III, married Katherine Swynford, legitimizing their long-standing relationship and their children. This marriage had significant political implications for English succession, as their children (the Beauforts) would later play crucial roles in English politics and the Tudor dynasty. The marriage reflected the changing social dynamics of the English nobility.
key_figures: Richard II (King of England, nephew of Gaunt)
events.certainty_note: Well-documented in English royal records and contemporary chronicles. The date is firmly established.

events.title: Hongwu Emperor's Compilation of the Hongwu Code
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor continued refinement of the legal code that would become the foundation of Ming Dynasty law. The code emphasized Confucian principles, centralized imperial authority, and harsh punishments for corruption. This legal framework would influence Chinese governance for centuries and represented the Hongwu Emperor's effort to establish a stable, bureaucratic state after the collapse of Mongol Yuan rule.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Code was compiled over several years (1370s-1390s). Attribution to 1386 specifically is approximate; the code was finalized and promulgated in 1397.

events.title: Zheng He's Early Career in Ming Service
events.description: Zheng He, the future admiral and explorer, was serving in the household of the Yongle Prince (later the Yongle Emperor) during this period. Though his famous voyages would not begin until 1405, his rise in the Ming court during the 1380s-1390s established the connections and authority that would enable his later expeditions. This period saw his consolidation of power and influence within the imperial household.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Later biographical compilations on Zheng He

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's early career is less well-documented than his later voyages. The exact timeline of his rise is inferred from later sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Pločnik's date is disputed in some sources, with alternative dates of 1385 or 1387 appearing in different Ottoman and Serbian chronicles. The significance of this battle has sometimes been overstated in nationalist historiography; while important, it was one of several Ottoman victories rather than a decisive turning point. Modern scholarship emphasizes that Ottoman expansion was gradual rather than a sudden conquest.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1386 is uneven. European events are well-recorded in chronicles and royal records, particularly for England and the Ottoman-Balkan frontier. Chinese sources are rich and contemporary. However, the Ottoman sources are primarily later compilations, introducing dating uncertainty. African, American, and Oceanic histories are largely absent from surviving written records for this year. The surviving sources reflect elite, court-centered perspectives and may underrepresent popular movements, economic conditions, and non-literate societies.
