events.title: Battle of Pločnik (alternative dating)
events.description: Some sources date the Ottoman victory at Pločnik to 1385 rather than 1386. Ottoman forces under Murat I defeated Serbian forces, advancing Ottoman control in the Balkans. The battle demonstrated Ottoman military superiority and was part of the broader conquest of Serbian territories.
events.certainty_note: Dating of this battle is disputed across sources. Different Ottoman and Serbian chronicles place it in 1385, 1386, or 1387. Modern scholarship has not definitively resolved this discrepancy.

events.title: Portuguese Expedition to Morocco (Ceuta campaign preparations)
events.description: Portuguese forces under King John I began preparations for the conquest of Ceuta, a major North African port city held by the Marinid dynasty. Though the actual conquest would occur in 1415, the 1385 period saw significant military and naval preparations, including the construction of ships and gathering of forces. This campaign represented the beginning of Portuguese expansion into North Africa and the Atlantic.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (young, not yet leading expeditions)

sources.name: Zurara's Chronicle of the Discovery and Conquest of Guinea

events.certainty_note: The preparations for Ceuta are documented in Portuguese sources, though the exact timeline is somewhat unclear. The campaign was delayed by other conflicts.

events.title: Hongwu Emperor's Purge of Officials
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor continued his systematic purges of officials suspected of disloyalty or corruption. These purges, which lasted throughout the 1380s, resulted in thousands of executions and were part of the emperor's effort to consolidate absolute power and eliminate potential rivals. The purges created a climate of fear in the bureaucracy and contributed to the centralization of Ming authority.
key_figures: Various officials and generals (victims of purges)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: The purges of the 1380s are well-documented in Ming records. The scale and brutality are confirmed by multiple contemporary sources.

events.title: Richard II's Consolidation of Power in England
events.description: King Richard II, who had ascended to the throne in 1377 at age 10, was consolidating his personal authority in the mid-1380s. The period saw increasing tension between the young king and the magnates who had served as regents and advisors. Richard's efforts to assert royal prerogative would eventually lead to the conflicts of 1397-1399.
key_figures: John of Gaunt (Duke of Lancaster, uncle)
Thomas of Woodstock (Duke of Gloucester, uncle)

sources.name: English royal records and parliamentary rolls

events.certainty_note: Richard II's political activities in 1385 are well-documented in English chronicles and parliamentary records.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Battle of Pločnik to 1385 is disputed; some sources place it in 1386 or 1387. The significance of Portuguese preparations for Ceuta in 1385 is uncertain; the campaign may have been less organized at this date than later sources suggest. Richard II's consolidation of power in 1385 was gradual and contested; the narrative of a young king asserting authority is somewhat simplified, as he remained dependent on magnates for several more years.
historiographic_note: Sources for 1385 are moderate in quality and coverage. English and Chinese sources are relatively rich. Ottoman and Serbian sources are primarily later compilations with dating uncertainties. Portuguese sources exist but are sometimes retrospectively organized around the later conquest of Ceuta. African, American, and Oceanic histories remain absent from written records. The surviving sources reflect elite political and military perspectives.

events.title: Death of John Wycliffe
events.description: John Wycliffe, the English theologian and reformer, died in December 1384. Wycliffe had been a major critic of papal authority and church corruption, advocating for the translation of the Bible into English and challenging clerical wealth. Though he died of natural causes and was not persecuted during his lifetime, his ideas would profoundly influence the Lollard movement and later Protestant reformers. His death marked the end of his direct influence but the beginning of his legacy.
key_figures: Richard II (King of England, patron of Wycliffe)

sources.name: Lollard chronicles and later Protestant sources

events.certainty_note: Wycliffe's death in December 1384 is well-documented in contemporary English chronicles and university records.

events.title: Hongwu Emperor's Establishment of the Jinyiwei (Embroidered Uniform Guard)
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor established or reorganized the Jinyiwei, a secret police and intelligence agency that would become notorious for its surveillance and enforcement of imperial will. The Jinyiwei operated outside normal bureaucratic channels and reported directly to the emperor, enabling him to monitor officials and suppress dissent. This institution became a defining feature of Ming absolutism and would persist throughout the dynasty.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: The establishment of the Jinyiwei is traditionally dated to 1384, though some sources suggest it was reorganized or expanded at this time rather than newly created. The exact date is somewhat uncertain.

events.title: Ottoman Conquest of Sofia
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murat I conquered Sofia, the capital of Bulgaria, from the Bulgarian Empire. This conquest marked a major step in Ottoman expansion into the Balkans and the decline of Bulgarian independence. Sofia would remain under Ottoman control for nearly five centuries. The conquest demonstrated Ottoman military superiority and the fragmentation of Balkan Christian states.

sources.name: Bulgarian historical tradition and historiographic consensus

events.certainty_note: The conquest of Sofia is attested in Ottoman and Bulgarian sources, but contemporary documentation is limited. The date of 1384 is accepted by most modern scholars, though some sources place it slightly earlier or later.
cross_references: 1389-001 (Battle of Kosovo, continuation of Ottoman expansion)

events.title: Marriage of Margaret III of Male and John I of Portugal
events.description: The marriage of Margaret III, Countess of Male (Flanders), to John I of Portugal was arranged to strengthen diplomatic ties between Portugal and Flanders. Though the marriage did not produce lasting political consequences, it represented the diplomatic networks of late medieval Europe and Portugal's efforts to establish international alliances.
events.certainty_note: The marriage is attested in Portuguese and Flemish records, though the exact date may be approximate (±1 year).

disconfirming_evidence: The date of the Ottoman conquest of Sofia is disputed in some sources, with alternative dates of 1382, 1385, or 1386 appearing in different Ottoman chronicles. The significance of Wycliffe's death has sometimes been overstated; while he was influential, his direct impact on religious reform was limited during his lifetime, and the Lollard movement developed somewhat independently of his direct teachings. The marriage of Margaret III and John I may not have occurred in 1384; some sources suggest 1385 or other dates.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1384 is moderate. English sources are relatively rich, particularly regarding Wycliffe. Chinese sources are contemporary and detailed. Ottoman and Bulgarian sources are primarily later compilations with dating uncertainties. Portuguese sources exist but are sometimes sparse for this period. African, American, and Oceanic histories remain absent from written records. The surviving sources reflect elite, court-centered perspectives and may underrepresent popular religious movements and economic conditions.

events.title: Portuguese Crisis of Succession (1383-1385)
events.description: Following the death of King Ferdinand I in 1383, Portugal entered a period of succession crisis. Ferdinand's widow, Queen Leonor Teles, attempted to maintain power and secure the throne for her daughter Beatrice (who was married to the King of Castile). This crisis led to civil conflict and the eventual triumph of John of Aviz (John I), who established the Aviz Dynasty. The crisis lasted until 1385 but began with Ferdinand's death in 1383.

sources.name: Fernão Lopes' Chronicle of John I

events.certainty_note: The Portuguese succession crisis is well-documented in Portuguese chronicles and records. The date of Ferdinand's death (November 1383) is firmly established.

events.title: Hongwu Emperor's Continued Consolidation of Ming Power
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor continued his systematic consolidation of power through administrative reforms, purges of officials, and the establishment of centralized bureaucratic control. The 1380s saw the emperor at the height of his power, implementing policies that would define Ming governance for centuries. His efforts to eliminate potential rivals and establish absolute imperial authority were largely successful by this period.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's consolidation of power in the 1380s is well-documented in Ming records.

events.title: Ottoman Expansion in the Balkans
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murat I continued their expansion into the Balkans, consolidating control over territories conquered in previous years. The period 1383-1389 saw the Ottoman Empire at the height of its Balkan expansion, with the empire controlling much of modern-day Bulgaria, Serbia, and parts of Greece. This expansion was facilitated by the fragmentation of Balkan Christian states and Ottoman military superiority.

sources.name: Balkan historical tradition and historiographic consensus

events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans during this period is attested in Ottoman and Balkan sources, though specific events and dates are sometimes uncertain.

events.title: Richard II's Early Reign in England
events.description: King Richard II, who had ascended to the throne in 1377 at age 10, was in his early reign in 1383. The period saw the young king gradually asserting his authority, though he remained dependent on magnates and advisors. The Peasants' Revolt of 1381 had been suppressed, and Richard was consolidating his position as king. His reign would be marked by increasing tension with the nobility.
key_figures: John of Gaunt (Duke of Lancaster, uncle and advisor)
Thomas of Woodstock (Duke of Gloucester, uncle)

sources.name: English royal records and parliamentary rolls

events.certainty_note: Richard II's activities in 1383 are documented in English chronicles and parliamentary records.

disconfirming_evidence: The Portuguese succession crisis is sometimes portrayed as a simple conflict between John of Aviz and Castilian interests, but the reality was more complex, with multiple factions and interests involved. The significance of Ottoman expansion in the Balkans has sometimes been overstated in nationalist historiography; Ottoman conquest was gradual and faced significant resistance. Richard II's position in 1383 was more precarious than sometimes portrayed; he was still heavily dependent on magnates and had not yet consolidated personal authority.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1383 is moderate. Portuguese sources are relatively rich regarding the succession crisis. English sources are detailed regarding Richard II. Chinese sources are contemporary and comprehensive. Ottoman and Balkan sources are primarily later compilations with dating uncertainties. African, American, and Oceanic histories remain absent from written records. The surviving sources reflect elite, court-centered perspectives and may underrepresent popular movements and economic conditions.

graph_edges.note: Portuguese succession crisis led to the establishment of the Aviz Dynasty
Ottoman expansion in the Balkans continued toward the Battle of Kosovo
Hongwu Emperor's consolidation of power through institutional reforms
Richard II's early reign led to his consolidation of power

era_context: Late medieval Europe with Ottoman expansion in the Balkans, Ming Dynasty consolidating power in China, and England under the young King Richard II. The Hundred Years' War continues with shifting fortunes. The Iberian Peninsula remains fragmented between Christian kingdoms and the Nasrid Emirate of Granada. Religious tensions are building in Central Europe.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and the Steppe (sparse documentation)
Oceania and Pacific islands (minimal written records)
Americas (no written records in European tradition)

events.title: Hongwu Emperor's Abolition of the Chancellery
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor abolished the position of Chancellor (Zaixiang), a major administrative office that had existed since ancient times. This action was part of the emperor's effort to consolidate absolute power and prevent any official from accumulating too much authority. The abolition of the Chancellery marked a significant shift toward personal imperial rule and the direct administration of the empire by the emperor himself, with the assistance of the Grand Secretariat.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: The abolition of the Chancellery in 1382 is well-documented in Ming records. This was a major institutional change with significant consequences for Ming governance.

events.title: Ottoman Conquest of Nish (Niš)
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murat I conquered Nish (Niš), a major Serbian fortress city. The conquest of Nish was a significant Ottoman victory and marked the consolidation of Ottoman control over central Serbia. The city would remain under Ottoman control for nearly five centuries. The conquest demonstrated Ottoman military superiority and the fragmentation of Serbian resistance.

sources.name: Serbian historical tradition and historiographic consensus

events.certainty_note: The conquest of Nish is attested in Ottoman and Serbian sources, but contemporary documentation is limited. The date of 1382 is accepted by most modern scholars, though some sources place it in 1381 or 1383.

events.title: Marriage of Mary of Hungary and Sigismund of Luxembourg
events.description: The marriage of Mary of Hungary to Sigismund of Luxembourg was arranged to strengthen diplomatic ties between Hungary and the Holy Roman Empire. Sigismund would later become King of Hungary and Holy Roman Emperor, and this marriage was part of the dynastic politics of Central Europe. The marriage represented the complex web of alliances among Central European powers.
key_figures: Sigismund of Luxembourg (future King of Hungary and Holy Roman Emperor)
events.certainty_note: The marriage is attested in Hungarian and Holy Roman Empire records, though the exact date may be approximate (±1 year).

events.title: Richard II's Consolidation of Royal Authority
events.description: King Richard II, now in his mid-teens, was gradually consolidating his authority as king. The period saw the young king asserting his will in court and parliament, though he remained dependent on magnates for military and political support. The suppression of the Peasants' Revolt in 1381 had strengthened his position, and he was beginning to develop the personal authority that would characterize his later reign.
key_figures: John of Gaunt (Duke of Lancaster, uncle and advisor)

sources.name: English royal records and parliamentary rolls

events.certainty_note: Richard II's activities in 1382 are documented in English chronicles and parliamentary records.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of the Ottoman conquest of Nish is disputed in some sources, with alternative dates of 1381, 1382, or 1383 appearing in different Ottoman chronicles. The significance of the conquest has sometimes been overstated; while important, it was one of several Ottoman victories rather than a decisive turning point. The abolition of the Chancellery by the Hongwu Emperor is sometimes portrayed as a sudden, dramatic action, but it was part of a gradual process of consolidating imperial power that had been underway for several years.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1382 is moderate. Chinese sources are contemporary and detailed regarding the Hongwu Emperor's reforms. English sources are relatively rich regarding Richard II. Ottoman and Serbian sources are primarily later compilations with dating uncertainties. Hungarian and Central European sources exist but are sometimes sparse for this period. African, American, and Oceanic histories remain absent from written records. The surviving sources reflect elite, court-centered perspectives and may underrepresent popular movements and economic conditions.

graph_edges.note: Abolition of Chancellery was part of Hongwu Emperor's consolidation of power
Ottoman conquest of Nish was part of the campaign leading to the conquest of Sofia
Ottoman expansion in the Balkans continued toward the Battle of Kosovo
Richard II's consolidation of authority continued in subsequent years

era_context: Late 14th-century Europe during the Hundred Years' War between England and France; the Ottoman Empire expanding in the Balkans; Ming Dynasty consolidating control in China after overthrowing Mongol Yuan; the Papacy in Avignon transitioning toward the Western Schism. This is a period of political fragmentation, religious upheaval, and military conflict across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records from this period)

events.title: Peasants' Revolt in England
events.description: A major uprising of peasants and artisans across southeastern England, triggered by the poll tax and serfdom grievances. Led by Wat Tyler, Jack Straw, and others, the revolt saw rebels march on London, storm the Tower of London, and execute several nobles and officials including Archbishop Sudbury. King Richard II, then 14, negotiated with the rebels, granting charters of freedom that were later revoked. The revolt was suppressed by late summer, with Tyler killed in negotiations at Smithfield. Though ultimately defeated, the uprising demonstrated the power of organized lower-class resistance and contributed to the gradual decline of serfdom in England.
events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary and near-contemporary chronicles document the revolt in detail. Dates and key events are well-established.
cross_references: Part of broader 14th-century peasant unrest across Europe

events.title: Battle of the Bileća (Ottoman-Bosnian conflict)
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murat I engaged Bosnian and Serbian forces in the region of Bileća in Herzegovina. This conflict was part of the Ottoman expansion into the Balkans during the 1380s. The exact outcome and significance of this specific engagement is debated by modern historians; some sources emphasize Ottoman advances, while others note the continued resistance of Balkan Christian states. The broader campaign contributed to Ottoman consolidation of control over the region.

sources.name: Bosnian and Serbian sources (fragmentary, later recorded)

events.certainty_note: Ottoman-Balkan conflicts of this period are documented primarily in later Ottoman chronicles and fragmentary Balkan sources. Exact dating and outcomes of individual engagements are often uncertain.

events.title: Zhu Yuanzhang consolidates Ming control; purges of officials
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang), founder of the Ming Dynasty, continued his consolidation of power through purges of officials and military commanders he deemed disloyal or threatening. In 1381, he initiated major purges of the bureaucracy and military, executing thousands of officials and generals. These purges were part of his broader effort to centralize authority and eliminate potential rivals. The Hongwu Emperor's paranoia and authoritarian methods shaped Ming governance for decades and contributed to a climate of fear within the imperial administration.
key_figures: Zhu Yuanzhang / Hongwu Emperor (Ming founder and ruler)
Hu Weiyong (executed official, 1380, but purges continued in 1381)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records, official court records)

events.certainty_note: Ming court records and official histories document the purges in detail. Dates and scale are well-established, though motivations and justifications are presented from the imperial perspective.

events.title: Papal conclave and Urban VI's continued reign
events.description: Pope Urban VI remained in Rome, consolidating his position as the legitimate pope following the return of the papacy from Avignon in 1377. Urban VI's abrasive personality and authoritarian style had alienated many cardinals, but by 1381 his position was relatively secure in Rome, though the Western Schism would erupt in 1378 (with the election of the rival Clement VII). Urban VI's reign was marked by conflict with the Papal States' nobility and attempts to reform the Church, though his methods were often harsh and counterproductive.
key_figures: Pope Urban VI (Bartolomeo Prignano, reigning pope)

sources.name: Papal registers and contemporary chronicles (Dietrich of Nieheim, others)
Liber Pontificalis and later papal histories

events.certainty_note: Papal records and contemporary chronicles document Urban VI's reign. The Western Schism is well-documented, though interpretations of legitimacy vary by Catholic and Orthodox tradition.
cross_references: Follows from 1377-001 (return of papacy to Rome)

events.title: Zheng He's early career in Ming service (approximate)
events.description: Zheng He, the future Ming admiral and explorer, was in his early career serving the Hongwu Emperor's military. Born around 1371, Zheng He would have been approximately 10 years old in 1381, likely already in imperial service or training. His rise to prominence would come later under the Yongle Emperor (r. 1402–1424), but his early years in the 1380s were formative for his later achievements. This entry is included for context rather than as a specific event.

sources.name: Later Ming histories and Zheng He biographies

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's exact birth year and early career details are not precisely documented. Dates are approximate based on later biographical compilations.

disconfirming_evidence: The Peasants' Revolt is sometimes portrayed as a proto-democratic uprising, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the rebels' demands were often conservative (restoration of older rights, not universal equality) and that the revolt's long-term impact on serfdom was less direct than once believed—serfdom declined gradually over the 15th century due to labor scarcity and economic factors, not primarily because of the 1381 uprising. The Western Schism's origins are sometimes attributed solely to Urban VI's personality, but modern historians emphasize structural factors in the Church and the cardinals' political interests. Ottoman expansion in the Balkans is sometimes presented as inevitable, but recent scholarship notes the resilience and agency of Balkan states in negotiating, resisting, and adapting to Ottoman pressure.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1381 is rich for Western Europe (England, France, Papal States) and moderately good for the Ottoman Empire and Ming China, though Ottoman sources are primarily later compilations. Sub-Saharan Africa, interior Southeast Asia, and the Americas are essentially absent from contemporary written records available to modern historians. The Peasants' Revolt is heavily documented from English and French chronicles, but these sources reflect elite perspectives; peasant voices are largely absent. Ming records are official court documents, which present the Hongwu Emperor's perspective. Ottoman sources are later compilations, introducing dating uncertainty. The historical record for 1381 is shaped by the survival of written records in literate, centralized states; oral traditions and non-literate societies are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Both represent major political upheaval in their respective regions during the same year
Part of broader 14th-century peasant and lower-class unrest across Europe

era_context: Late 14th-century Europe in the midst of the Hundred Years' War; the Ottoman Empire consolidating power in the Balkans; Ming Dynasty in its early consolidation phase under the Hongwu Emperor; the Papacy recently returned to Rome from Avignon, with the Western Schism imminent. This is a period of military conflict, political centralization, and religious transition.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pacific Islands and Oceania (minimal contemporary records)

events.title: Poll Tax and Social Unrest in England
events.description: The English Parliament imposed a poll tax (head tax) on all adults, a highly unpopular measure that fell heavily on the poor and working classes. The tax was collected in 1380 and sparked widespread resentment and resistance, particularly in southeastern England. Collectors faced hostility and refusal to pay. This tax was a direct cause of the Peasants' Revolt that erupted in 1381. The poll tax represented an attempt by the English crown to fund the Hundred Years' War, but it exposed the limits of royal authority over the commons and demonstrated the political power of organized resistance.
key_figures: John of Gaunt (Duke of Lancaster, regent)
events.certainty_note: Parliamentary records and contemporary chronicles document the poll tax and its collection in 1380. The date and impact are well-established.

events.title: Purge of Hu Weiyong and associates in Ming China
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor executed Hu Weiyong, a high-ranking official and chancellor, along with thousands of associates, on charges of treason and conspiracy. Hu Weiyong had been one of the most powerful officials in the early Ming government, but the Hongwu Emperor grew suspicious of his influence and power. The purge was part of the emperor's broader effort to centralize authority and eliminate potential rivals. The execution of Hu Weiyong and the subsequent purges of his associates marked a turning point in Ming governance, establishing a pattern of imperial paranoia and authoritarian control that would characterize the Hongwu Emperor's later reign.
events.certainty_note: Ming court records document the purge in detail. The date and scale are well-established, though the charges against Hu Weiyong are presented from the imperial perspective.

events.title: Battle of the Maritsa River (Ottoman-Serbian conflict)
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murat I defeated Serbian forces in the region of the Maritsa River in what is now Bulgaria and Greece. This battle was part of the Ottoman expansion into the Balkans and the gradual conquest of Serbian and Bulgarian territories. The exact date and significance of this engagement are debated by historians; some sources place it in 1380, others in 1382 or nearby years. The battle contributed to Ottoman consolidation of control over the southern Balkans and the decline of Serbian power in the region.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman-Balkan conflicts of this period are documented primarily in later Ottoman chronicles. Exact dating and outcomes are uncertain; some sources place this battle in 1382 or other nearby years.

events.title: Clement VII elected as antipope; Western Schism begins
events.description: Following the death of Gregory XI in 1378 and the election of Urban VI, a group of cardinals (primarily French) elected Clement VII as a rival pope, claiming that Urban VI's election was invalid due to pressure from the Roman mob. Clement VII established his court in Avignon, while Urban VI remained in Rome. This split created the Western Schism, which would last until 1417. The schism reflected deeper political divisions between French and Italian interests, as well as disputes over papal authority and church governance. By 1380, the schism was well-established, with both popes claiming legitimacy and excommunicating each other.
key_figures: Clement VII (Avignon antipope, elected 1378, established by 1380)

sources.name: Dietrich of Nieheim and other contemporary chroniclers

events.certainty_note: The Western Schism is well-documented in papal records and contemporary chronicles. The election of Clement VII occurred in 1378, but the schism was fully established by 1380.
cross_references: Follows from 1378-001 (Urban VI's election and Clement VII's counter-election)

events.title: Tamerlane (Timur) consolidates power in Central Asia
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the Turco-Mongol conqueror, continued his consolidation of power in Central Asia and began his campaigns of conquest that would eventually extend from the Mediterranean to China. By 1380, Timur had established himself as the dominant power in Central Asia and was preparing for his major campaigns of conquest in the 1380s and 1390s. His rise represented a new phase of steppe imperialism and would have profound effects on the political geography of Asia.
key_figures: Various Central Asian and Persian rulers (rivals and vassals)

sources.name: Timur's own inscriptions and later biographical accounts
Persian and Central Asian chronicles (Rashid al-Din, others)

events.certainty_note: Timur's rise to power is documented in later Persian and Central Asian sources. The exact chronology of his early consolidation is somewhat uncertain, but by 1380 he was clearly the dominant power in Central Asia.

disconfirming_evidence: The poll tax is sometimes presented as the sole cause of the Peasants' Revolt, but modern historians emphasize that it was one of several grievances (including serfdom, labor scarcity, and social mobility) that contributed to the uprising. The Western Schism is sometimes portrayed as a simple dispute between Rome and Avignon, but modern scholarship emphasizes the complex political, theological, and institutional factors involved. Ottoman expansion is sometimes presented as inevitable, but recent scholarship notes the agency and resistance of Balkan states. Timur's rise is sometimes romanticized as a great conqueror, but modern historians emphasize the destructive impact of his campaigns and the instability they created.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1380 is rich for Western Europe (England, France, Papal States) and moderately good for the Ottoman Empire, Ming China, and Central Asia. However, Ottoman and Central Asian sources are primarily later compilations, introducing dating uncertainty. Sub-Saharan Africa, interior Southeast Asia, and the Americas are essentially absent from contemporary written records. The poll tax is documented in English parliamentary records and chronicles, but these reflect elite perspectives. Ming records are official court documents presenting the imperial perspective. The historical record for 1380 is shaped by the survival of written records in literate, centralized states; oral traditions and non-literate societies are underrepresented.

This is a period of military conflict, political centralization, and religious upheaval.

events.title: Treaty of Bruges between England and Burgundy
events.description: England and Burgundy negotiated a treaty (or truce) in 1379 as part of the broader diplomatic maneuvering during the Hundred Years' War. The treaty reflected the complex alliance system of the period, with Burgundy playing a crucial role as a buffer between English and French interests. The exact terms and significance of the 1379 treaty are debated by historians, but it represented an attempt to stabilize relations in the Low Countries and northern France during a period of military stalemate.
events.certainty_note: Diplomatic records and chronicles mention treaties and negotiations in 1379, but the exact terms and significance are sometimes unclear or disputed.
cross_references: Part of Hundred Years' War diplomacy (1337–1453)

events.title: Hongwu Emperor establishes provincial administration in Ming China
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor continued his consolidation of Ming power by establishing and reorganizing provincial administration. In 1379, he implemented administrative reforms that strengthened central control over the provinces and reduced the power of regional military commanders. These reforms were part of his broader effort to centralize authority and prevent the emergence of regional warlords. The administrative structure established in 1379 would form the basis of Ming provincial governance for centuries.
events.certainty_note: Ming court records document administrative reforms in 1379. The date and general nature of the reforms are well-established.

events.title: Ottoman siege of Scutari (Shkodër) in Albania
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murat I besieged the fortress city of Scutari (modern Shkodër) in Albania, which was held by Venetian forces. The siege was part of the Ottoman expansion into the Balkans and the competition between Ottoman and Venetian interests in the region. The siege lasted several years (1478–1479 in some sources, but Ottoman expansion in the region began in the 1370s–1380s). The exact dating of this siege is disputed; some sources place it in 1379, others in nearby years. The siege represented the Ottoman challenge to Venetian commercial and military dominance in the Balkans.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman-Venetian conflicts in the Balkans are documented, but the exact dating of individual sieges is often uncertain. Some sources place the Scutari siege in 1478–1479, not 1379.

events.title: Pope Urban VI consolidates authority in Rome
events.description: Pope Urban VI, having been elected in 1378, continued to consolidate his authority in Rome and the Papal States. By 1379, Urban VI had established himself as the legitimate pope in Rome, despite the rival claim of Clement VII in Avignon. Urban VI's reign was marked by conflict with the Papal States' nobility and attempts to reform the Church, though his authoritarian and often harsh methods alienated many cardinals and clergy. The Western Schism, initiated in 1378, was now fully established, with both popes claiming legitimacy.
key_figures: Pope Urban VI (Bartolomeo Prignano, reigning pope)

sources.name: Dietrich of Nieheim and other contemporary chroniclers

events.certainty_note: Papal records and contemporary chronicles document Urban VI's reign and consolidation of authority in Rome.

events.title: Timur defeats Tokhtamysh and consolidates Central Asian power
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane) defeated Tokhtamysh, a rival Central Asian ruler, in a series of conflicts that consolidated Timur's dominance in Central Asia. The exact dating of these conflicts is uncertain, with some sources placing key battles in 1379, others in nearby years. Timur's victories over Tokhtamysh and other rivals established him as the supreme power in Central Asia and set the stage for his major campaigns of conquest in the 1380s and 1390s. These conflicts also affected the Golden Horde and the political balance of power on the Eurasian steppe.

sources.name: Persian and Central Asian chronicles (Rashid al-Din, others)

events.certainty_note: Timur's conflicts with Tokhtamysh are documented in later Persian and Central Asian sources. The exact chronology is uncertain, with different sources placing key events in different years.
cross_references: Part of Timur's rise to power (1370s–1380s)

disconfirming_evidence: The Treaty of Bruges is sometimes presented as a major diplomatic achievement, but modern historians note that it had limited practical impact and that the Hundred Years' War continued with little interruption. Ottoman expansion is sometimes presented as inevitable, but recent scholarship emphasizes the resistance and agency of Balkan and Venetian forces. Timur's rise is sometimes romanticized, but modern historians emphasize the destructive impact of his campaigns and the instability they created. The Western Schism is sometimes attributed to personal conflicts between popes, but modern scholarship emphasizes structural and political factors.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1379 is rich for Western Europe (England, France, Papal States) and moderately good for the Ottoman Empire, Ming China, and Central Asia. However, Ottoman and Central Asian sources are primarily later compilations, introducing dating uncertainty. Sub-Saharan Africa, interior Southeast Asia, and the Americas are essentially absent from contemporary written records. The historical record for 1379 is shaped by the survival of written records in literate, centralized states; oral traditions and non-literate societies are underrepresented. Diplomatic records are often fragmentary and may not capture the full complexity of negotiations.

era_context: Late 14th-century Europe during the Hundred Years' War between England and France; the Ottoman Empire expanding in the Balkans; Ming Dynasty consolidating control under the Hongwu Emperor; the Papacy returning to Rome from Avignon after nearly 70 years in France. This is a period of military conflict, political centralization, and religious upheaval.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pacific Islands and Oceania (minimal contemporary records)

events.title: Election of Pope Urban VI and beginning of Western Schism
events.description: Following the death of Pope Gregory XI in March 1378, the College of Cardinals elected Bartolomeo Prignano as Pope Urban VI. Urban VI was a harsh and authoritarian figure who quickly alienated many cardinals, particularly the French cardinals who had dominated the papacy during the Avignon period. In September 1378, a group of cardinals (primarily French) declared Urban VI's election invalid, claiming it had been made under pressure from the Roman mob, and elected Clement VII as a rival pope. Clement VII established his court in Avignon, while Urban VI remained in Rome. This split initiated the Western Schism, which would last until 1417 and profoundly divided the Catholic Church. The schism reflected deeper political divisions between French and Italian interests, as well as disputes over papal authority and church governance.
key_figures: Pope Urban VI (Bartolomeo Prignano, elected April 1378)
Clement VII (Robert of Geneva, elected September 1378 as antipope)

sources.name: Dietrich of Nieheim, De Schismate (contemporary account)

events.certainty_note: The Western Schism is one of the most well-documented events of the medieval Church. Papal records, contemporary chronicles, and later histories all confirm the dates and key figures involved.
cross_references: Leads to 1417-001 (Council of Constance ends schism)

events.title: Hongwu Emperor establishes the Jinyiwei (Embroidered Uniform Guard)
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor established the Jinyiwei (Embroidered Uniform Guard), a secret police and intelligence agency that would become one of the most feared institutions in Ming China. The Jinyiwei was created to investigate treason, suppress dissent, and enforce the emperor's will. The establishment of this agency in 1378 marked a turning point in Ming governance, institutionalizing surveillance and repression as tools of imperial control. The Jinyiwei would play a major role in the purges and political upheavals of the Hongwu Emperor's later reign.
events.certainty_note: Ming court records document the establishment of the Jinyiwei in 1378. The date and general nature of the institution are well-established.

events.title: Ottoman forces consolidate control in southern Balkans
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murat I continued their expansion in the Balkans, consolidating control over territories in what is now Bulgaria, Greece, and Albania. In 1378, Ottoman forces were engaged in multiple campaigns against Serbian, Bulgarian, and Venetian forces. The exact details of specific battles and sieges in 1378 are often unclear, as Ottoman sources are primarily later compilations. However, by 1378, the Ottoman Empire had clearly established itself as the dominant military power in the Balkans and was continuing its systematic conquest of the region.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans is well-documented in broad outline, but specific events in 1378 are less clearly dated. Ottoman sources are primarily later compilations.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English military campaigns in France
events.description: English forces, under the command of various nobles, continued military campaigns in France as part of the Hundred Years' War. In 1378, English forces were engaged in raids and sieges in Aquitaine and other regions of southwestern France. The campaigns of 1378 were part of the broader pattern of English military activity during this period, though the war had entered a phase of relative stalemate compared to the major victories of the 1350s–1360s. The exact details of specific campaigns in 1378 are often unclear, but English forces remained active in France.
key_figures: King Edward III (England, died 1377, but his legacy dominated)
events.certainty_note: The Hundred Years' War is well-documented, but specific campaigns in 1378 are less clearly detailed in surviving sources.

events.title: Timur consolidates power in Central Asia
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the Turco-Mongol conqueror, was consolidating his power in Central Asia in 1378. By this year, Timur had established himself as the dominant power in the region and was preparing for his major campaigns of conquest that would begin in the 1380s. The exact chronology of Timur's early rise is uncertain, as sources are primarily later compilations, but by 1378 he was clearly emerging as a major power in Central Asia.

sources.name: Persian and Central Asian chronicles (Rashid al-Din, others)

events.certainty_note: Timur's early rise to power is documented in later Persian and Central Asian sources. The exact chronology is uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The Western Schism is sometimes presented as a simple dispute between Rome and Avignon, but modern scholarship emphasizes the complex political, theological, and institutional factors involved. Urban VI's personality is sometimes blamed for the schism, but modern historians note that the schism reflected deeper structural problems in the Church and the cardinals' political interests. The Hongwu Emperor's establishment of the Jinyiwei is sometimes presented as a rational security measure, but modern historians emphasize its role in creating a climate of fear and repression. Ottoman expansion is sometimes presented as inevitable, but recent scholarship notes the resistance and agency of Balkan states.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1378 is rich for Western Europe (England, France, Papal States) and moderately good for the Ottoman Empire and Ming China. However, Ottoman and Central Asian sources are primarily later compilations, introducing dating uncertainty. Sub-Saharan Africa, interior Southeast Asia, and the Americas are essentially absent from contemporary written records. The Western Schism is extensively documented in papal records and contemporary chronicles, making it one of the best-documented events of the year. The historical record for 1378 is shaped by the survival of written records in literate, centralized states; oral traditions and non-literate societies are underrepresented.

events.title: Return of the Papacy to Rome; Pope Gregory XI dies
events.description: Pope Gregory XI, who had returned the papal court to Rome in 1377 after nearly 70 years of papal residence in Avignon, died on March 27, 1377. Gregory XI's return to Rome was a major event, ending the so-called Avignon Papacy and reestablishing papal authority in Rome and the Papal States. However, Gregory XI's death immediately created a succession crisis. The College of Cardinals, under pressure from the Roman mob and the Roman nobility, elected Urban VI (Bartolomeo Prignano) as the new pope in April 1378. Gregory XI's death and the subsequent election of Urban VI set the stage for the Western Schism, which would erupt later in 1378.
key_figures: Pope Gregory XI (Pierre Roger de Beaufort, died March 27, 1377)

sources.name: Dietrich of Nieheim and other contemporary chroniclers

events.certainty_note: Gregory XI's death and the return of the papacy to Rome are well-documented in papal records and contemporary chronicles. The date of his death (March 27, 1377) is confirmed.
cross_references: Leads to 1378-001 (Urban VI's election and Western Schism)

events.title: Hongwu Emperor consolidates Ming control; administrative reforms
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor continued his consolidation of Ming power through administrative reforms and the elimination of potential rivals. In 1377, he implemented reforms that strengthened central control over the provinces and reduced the power of regional military commanders. These reforms were part of his broader effort to centralize authority and prevent the emergence of regional warlords. The administrative structure being established in 1377 would form the basis of Ming governance for centuries.
events.certainty_note: Ming court records document administrative reforms in 1377. The date and general nature of the reforms are well-established.

events.title: Ottoman forces expand in the Balkans
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murat I continued their expansion in the Balkans, consolidating control over territories in what is now Bulgaria, Greece, and Albania. In 1377, Ottoman forces were engaged in multiple campaigns against Serbian, Bulgarian, and Venetian forces. The exact details of specific battles and sieges in 1377 are often unclear, as Ottoman sources are primarily later compilations. However, by 1377, the Ottoman Empire had clearly established itself as the dominant military power in the Balkans and was continuing its systematic conquest of the region.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans is well-documented in broad outline, but specific events in 1377 are less clearly dated. Ottoman sources are primarily later compilations.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English military campaigns and diplomacy
events.description: English forces, under the command of various nobles, continued military campaigns and diplomatic negotiations in France as part of the Hundred Years' War. In 1377, English forces were engaged in raids and sieges in Aquitaine and other regions of southwestern France. The campaigns of 1377 were part of the broader pattern of English military activity during this period, though the war had entered a phase of relative stalemate compared to the major victories of the 1350s–1360s. The death of King Edward III in 1377 marked a transition in English leadership, with his young grandson Richard II becoming king.
key_figures: King Richard II (England, age 10, became king in 1377)
events.certainty_note: The death of Edward III on June 21, 1377, is well-documented. The Hundred Years' War is well-documented, though specific campaigns in 1377 are less clearly detailed.
cross_references: Leads to 1380-001 (poll tax crisis under Richard II)

events.title: Timur consolidates power in Central Asia
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the Turco-Mongol conqueror, was consolidating his power in Central Asia in 1377. By this year, Timur had established himself as a major power in the region and was preparing for his major campaigns of conquest that would begin in the 1380s. The exact chronology of Timur's early rise is uncertain, as sources are primarily later compilations, but by 1377 he was clearly emerging as a significant power in Central Asia.

sources.name: Persian and Central Asian chronicles (Rashid al-Din, others)

events.certainty_note: Timur's early rise to power is documented in later Persian and Central Asian sources. The exact chronology is uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The return of the papacy to Rome is sometimes presented as a triumphant restoration of papal authority, but modern historians emphasize that Gregory XI's return was politically motivated and that the papacy's position in Rome remained precarious. The Western Schism is sometimes attributed to Urban VI's personality alone, but modern scholarship emphasizes structural and political factors. The death of Edward III is sometimes presented as marking a clear transition in English history, but modern historians note the continuity of English policies and the gradual nature of change. Ottoman expansion is sometimes presented as inevitable, but recent scholarship notes the resistance and agency of Balkan states.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1377 is rich for Western Europe (England, France, Papal States) and moderately good for the Ottoman Empire and Ming China. However, Ottoman and Central Asian sources are primarily later compilations, introducing dating uncertainty. Sub-Saharan Africa, interior Southeast Asia, and the Americas are essentially absent from contemporary written records. The return of the papacy to Rome and Gregory XI's death are extensively documented in papal records and contemporary chronicles. The death of Edward III is well-documented in English and French chronicles. The historical record for 1377 is shaped by the survival of written records in literate, centralized states; oral traditions and non-literate societies are underrepresented.

era_context: The late 14th century in Europe is marked by the Western Schism in the Catholic Church (1378 onwards, but tensions building), the Hundred Years' War between England and France in its fourth decade, the rise of the Ottoman Empire in Anatolia and the Balkans, the Ming Dynasty consolidating power in China after overthrowing the Yuan, and the early Italian Renaissance beginning to flourish in city-states like Florence and Venice.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically documented in Western sources)
Interior Americas (Aztec and Inca empires well-documented in their own records, but European sources sparse)

events.title: Death of Pope Gregory XI; return of papacy to Rome
events.description: Pope Gregory XI died on 27 March 1376 in Rome, ending the Avignon Papacy (1309–1377). His death precipitated a succession crisis that would lead directly to the Western Schism. The cardinals, under pressure from Roman mobs demanding an Italian pope, elected Urban VI in April 1378 (not yet in 1376, but the groundwork was laid). Gregory's death marked the symbolic end of papal exile in Avignon and the beginning of institutional instability in the Church.
key_figures: Urban VI (elected 1378, but succession crisis began 1376)

sources.name: Liber Pontificalis; contemporary papal records and chronicles (Froissart, Villani)

events.certainty_note: Death date of Gregory XI is well-documented in papal records and contemporary chronicles. The date 27 March 1376 is standard in all major sources.
cross_references: 1378-001 (Western Schism begins with Urban VI's election)

events.title: Battle of Pločnik; Serbian defeat by Ottoman forces
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murad I defeated Serbian forces at Pločnik in 1376, marking a significant Ottoman advance into the Balkans. This battle demonstrated Ottoman military superiority and contributed to the gradual Ottoman conquest of Serbian territories. The defeat weakened Serbian resistance and accelerated Ottoman expansion northward into the Balkans, a process that would culminate in the Battle of Kosovo (1389).

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles (Neşri, Ashikpashazade); Serbian medieval sources

events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in Ottoman and Serbian sources, but dating varies slightly (some sources place it 1375–1377). 1376 is the most commonly cited date in modern scholarship, though ±1 year uncertainty is reasonable.
cross_references: 1389-001 (Battle of Kosovo, culmination of Ottoman Balkan expansion)

events.title: Zhu Yuanzhang consolidates Ming control; purge of early officials
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang), founder of the Ming Dynasty, continued consolidating power by purging officials and military commanders he deemed threats. In 1376, he initiated or intensified investigations into alleged conspiracies, particularly targeting the military elite. These purges were part of a broader pattern of centralizing authority and eliminating potential rivals, establishing the autocratic structure that would define Ming governance.
key_figures: Various purged officials (names recorded in Ming records)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming); official court records

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's purges occurred throughout the 1370s–1380s. Specific events in 1376 are recorded in Ming records, but the exact chronology of individual purges can be difficult to isolate without access to original Ming Shilu texts.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English campaigns in France; Treaty negotiations
events.description: In 1376, the Hundred Years' War continued with English forces conducting raids and campaigns in France. This year saw diplomatic negotiations and truces, including discussions that would lead to the Treaty of Bruges (1375) and subsequent peace efforts. The war remained in a phase of English military advantage, though the tide would shift in the following decades.

sources.name: Froissart's Chronicles; English and French royal records
Historiographic consensus on Hundred Years' War chronology

events.certainty_note: The broad outline of English-French military activity in 1376 is well-documented, though specific battles and engagements vary in detail across sources. The year falls within a well-attested period of the war.
cross_references: 1377-001 (Death of Edward III; succession of Richard II)

events.title: Peasants' Revolt in England: social unrest and labor demands
events.description: In 1376, England experienced significant social unrest related to labor conditions, taxation, and peasant grievances. This period saw the emergence of organized peasant resistance and demands for better conditions, which would culminate in the major Peasants' Revolt of 1381. The 1376 unrest reflected broader tensions over the Poll Tax, serfdom, and the power of the nobility.
key_figures: John of Gaunt (target of peasant anger)
Richard II (young king, not yet in full power)
events.certainty_note: The 1376 unrest is attested in chronicles, though it is often overshadowed by the 1381 Peasants' Revolt in historical narratives. The exact chronology and scale of 1376 events require careful reading of primary sources.

events.title: Tamerlane (Timur) campaigns in Central Asia and the Caucasus
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the Turco-Mongol conqueror, was in the midst of his campaigns to build a vast Central Asian empire. In 1376, he was consolidating control over Transoxiana and conducting military campaigns against rival khans and neighboring regions. These campaigns would eventually lead to his conquest of much of Asia and his invasion of the Ottoman Empire and India.
key_figures: Various Central Asian and Caucasian rulers

sources.name: Timurid chronicles (Yazdi, Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi); Persian and Islamic sources

events.certainty_note: Timur's campaigns are well-documented in Islamic and Persian sources, though exact dates of specific campaigns can vary. 1376 falls within his period of consolidation and expansion.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the 1376 peasant unrest in England is often minimized in favor of the 1381 Peasants' Revolt, which may obscure the continuity of social tension. Some sources attribute the 1376 unrest primarily to the Poll Tax, while others emphasize longer-term labor shortages and demographic changes post-Black Death. The Battle of Pločnik's exact date and significance are debated: some Ottoman sources place it earlier or later, and its strategic importance may have been exaggerated in later Ottoman historiography to support narratives of inevitable Ottoman expansion.
historiographic_note: The year 1376 is well-documented for Western Europe (England, France, Papal States) due to abundant chronicle sources. Ottoman and Serbian sources exist but are often later compilations. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are contemporary and detailed. Central Asian sources on Timur are rich but often written after his death. Sub-Saharan Africa, the interior Americas, and much of Southeast Asia have minimal written documentation for this specific year. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history require inference from broader patterns.

events.title: Treaty of Bruges; truce in the Hundred Years' War
events.description: The Treaty of Bruges, negotiated in 1375, established a truce between England and France in the Hundred Years' War. The treaty was brokered by Burgundy and represented a temporary cessation of major hostilities, though it did not resolve the underlying territorial disputes. The truce would hold intermittently until the war resumed with greater intensity in the following decades.
key_figures: Philip the Bold, Duke of Burgundy (mediator)

sources.name: Froissart's Chronicles; English and French royal records

events.certainty_note: The Treaty of Bruges is well-documented in contemporary diplomatic records and chronicles. The date 1375 is standard in all major sources.

events.title: Zheng He's early life; preparation for future voyages
events.description: In 1375, Zheng He was a young man in the service of the Hongwu Emperor's court. Though his famous voyages would not begin until 1405, this period saw his rise in the Ming military and administrative hierarchy. He was being groomed for the positions of authority that would later enable his unprecedented maritime expeditions.
key_figures: Zheng He (young official, not yet admiral)

sources.name: Ming Shilu; biographical records of Zheng He

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's exact activities in 1375 are not well-documented. This entry is based on the known timeline of his career and his rise in the Ming hierarchy during the 1370s–1380s.

events.title: Ottoman expansion in Anatolia; consolidation of Murad I's power
events.description: Murad I, the Ottoman Sultan, continued consolidating power in Anatolia and the Balkans in 1375. He was expanding Ottoman territory through military campaigns and diplomatic alliances, establishing the Ottoman Empire as a major regional power. This period saw the Ottoman state transition from a small principality to a significant empire.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in 1375 is attested in Ottoman sources, but specific events and dates can vary. The broader pattern of Murad I's consolidation is well-established.

events.title: Petrarch's influence on Italian Renaissance; humanist movement flourishing
events.description: In 1375, the humanist movement in Italy was flourishing, with Petrarch's influence (he died in 1374) continuing to shape intellectual life. Scholars and writers were increasingly turning to classical texts and emphasizing human potential and achievement. This period saw the early development of the Italian Renaissance, with Florence and Venice as major centers of cultural innovation.

sources.name: Humanist writings and correspondence; Salutati's letters

events.certainty_note: The flourishing of humanism in 1375 is attested in surviving writings and correspondence. The exact significance of 1375 as a turning point is a matter of historiographic interpretation.

events.title: Tamerlane's consolidation of power in Transoxiana
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane) was consolidating his control over Transoxiana and Central Asia in 1375, eliminating rival khans and establishing the Timurid Empire. This period saw the foundation of his vast empire, which would eventually stretch from Central Asia to the Mediterranean and the Indian subcontinent.

sources.name: Timurid chronicles (Yazdi, Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi)

events.certainty_note: Timur's consolidation of power is well-attested in Islamic and Persian sources, though specific events in 1375 are not always clearly dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The Treaty of Bruges is sometimes portrayed as a definitive peace, but it was actually a fragile truce that did not resolve underlying disputes and was frequently violated. The significance of the humanist movement in 1375 is partly a retrospective construction: contemporaries may not have recognized the 'Renaissance' as a distinct movement. Petrarch's death in 1374 is sometimes used as a symbolic endpoint for medieval culture, but this is a historiographic convenience rather than a sharp historical break.
historiographic_note: 1375 is well-documented for Western Europe, the Ottoman Empire, and China. Italian sources on the Renaissance are abundant. Ottoman and Central Asian sources exist but are often later compilations. The year is less well-documented for regions outside these areas. The surviving record emphasizes political and military events; cultural and economic history require inference from broader patterns.

events.title: Death of Petrarch; end of an era in Italian humanism
events.description: Francesco Petrarch, the Italian poet and humanist, died on 19 July 1374 in Arquà, near Padua. His death marked the end of a transformative intellectual life that had profoundly influenced the development of humanism and the early Renaissance. Petrarch's emphasis on classical texts, individual achievement, and the recovery of ancient learning shaped the intellectual landscape of the 14th and 15th centuries.

sources.name: Contemporary biographical accounts; Petrarch's own writings and correspondence

events.certainty_note: Petrarch's death date is well-documented. The date 19 July 1374 is standard in all major sources.

events.title: Hongwu Emperor's administrative reforms; centralization of Ming power
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang) continued implementing administrative reforms to centralize Ming power in 1374. He reorganized the bureaucracy, reduced the power of regional warlords, and established new systems of control. These reforms were part of his broader effort to create an autocratic state with power concentrated in the imperial center.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's administrative reforms are well-documented in Ming records, though specific reforms in 1374 require careful reading of the Ming Shilu.

This period saw English military dominance, with campaigns led by John of Gaunt and other English commanders. The war remained in a phase of relative English advantage, though the underlying territorial disputes remained unresolved.

sources.name: Froissart's Chronicles; English and French royal records

events.title: Battle of La Rochelle; English naval defeat
events.description: On 22 June 1372, an English naval fleet was defeated by a Castilian-French fleet at La Rochelle. The battle was a significant naval engagement in the Hundred Years' War, resulting in the loss of English ships and personnel. The defeat marked a turning point in English naval dominance and demonstrated the growing power of French and Castilian naval forces.

In Europe, the Hundred Years' War between England and France continued; the Ottoman Empire was expanding in the Balkans; the Ming Dynasty had recently consolidated power in China (1368); and the Timurid Renaissance was beginning in Central Asia. This was a period of significant political realignment, religious reform movements, and early maritime exploration.

events.title: Battle of Maritsa (Meriç River)
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murad I defeated a Serbian-Bulgarian coalition, decisively establishing Ottoman dominance in the southern Balkans. This victory marked a turning point in Ottoman expansion into Europe and led to the subjugation of Bulgarian and Serbian territories. The battle demonstrated the military superiority of Ottoman cavalry tactics and accelerated the decline of Byzantine and Balkan Christian states.
events.certainty_note: The battle is well-attested in Ottoman and Serbian sources, though contemporary documentation is limited. The date 1371 is accepted by modern scholarship, though some sources place it in 1372–1373.

events.title: Zheng He's early career in Ming service
events.description: Zheng He, a Muslim eunuch of Mongol descent, was rising in prominence at the Ming court under the Yongle Emperor's patronage. Though his famous voyages would not begin until 1405, his appointment to positions of military and administrative authority in the early 1370s laid the groundwork for his later role as commander of the treasure fleets. This period saw his integration into the imperial bureaucracy.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Ming Emperor, r. 1402–1424, but rising in influence)

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Zheng He's early career

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's exact career trajectory in the early 1370s is not precisely documented. He was born c. 1371, so this entry reflects his emergence into historical record rather than a specific datable event.

events.title: Timur (Tamerlane) consolidates power in Samarkand
events.description: Timur, the Turco-Mongol conqueror, was consolidating his control over Central Asia and establishing Samarkand as his capital. By 1371, he had defeated rival khans and was beginning the military campaigns that would create one of history's largest empires. This period marked the beginning of the Timurid Renaissance, which would profoundly influence Islamic art, architecture, and culture.

sources.name: Timurid chronicles and later compilations (e.g., Zafar-nama by Sharaf ad-Din Ali Yazdi)

events.certainty_note: Timur's consolidation of power occurred over several years in the late 1360s–early 1370s. The exact chronology is somewhat uncertain due to reliance on later sources, but 1371 is a reasonable approximation for his establishment of Samarkand as capital.
cross_references: 1380-001 (Timur's campaigns in the Caucasus and Persia)

events.title: Continued Hundred Years' War: English campaigns in France
events.description: The Hundred Years' War between England and France continued with English forces conducting raids and military campaigns in France. In 1371, English power was beginning to wane after the death of Edward the Black Prince (1376), though English forces still held significant territories in Aquitaine and Gascony. This period saw the transition from the dominance of English chivalric warfare to a more defensive posture.
events.certainty_note: The Hundred Years' War is extensively documented in contemporary chronicles and administrative records. The year 1371 is well-attested, though specific military engagements may vary by source.

events.title: Papal Schism deepens in Western Christianity
events.description: The Western Schism (1378–1417) was in its early stages, with competing papal claimants in Rome and Avignon. By 1371, the papacy had returned to Rome from Avignon (1377), but tensions over papal authority and reform were mounting. This period saw increasing calls for church reform and the emergence of figures like John Wycliffe in England, who challenged papal authority and advocated for vernacular scripture.

sources.name: Wycliffe's writings and later biographical accounts

events.certainty_note: The religious tensions of this period are well-documented in ecclesiastical records and contemporary writings. The year 1371 is clearly attested.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Maritsa is sometimes dated to 1372 or 1373 in some sources, creating minor chronological uncertainty. Additionally, the significance of this battle has been somewhat overstated in older historiography; while it was important, it did not immediately lead to the complete subjugation of the Balkans, which occurred more gradually over the following decades. Zheng He's birth year is sometimes given as 1371 and sometimes as other years in the 1370s, reflecting uncertainty in Ming records. The Western Schism is often dated to 1378, not 1371, though the tensions underlying it were present throughout the 1370s.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1371 is uneven. European events (Hundred Years' War, Ottoman expansion, religious tensions) are well-documented in contemporary chronicles and administrative records. Ottoman and Central Asian events rely more heavily on later compilations, particularly Timurid chronicles written in the 15th–16th centuries. Chinese records are detailed but often focus on court politics rather than broader social history. African, American, and Oceanic history for this year is almost entirely absent from surviving written records, reflecting both the limitations of literacy in these regions and the Eurocentric bias of historical preservation. The sources that do survive are often shaped by court patronage, religious authority, and the interests of literate elites.

graph_edges.note: Ottoman victory at Maritsa set the stage for further Balkan expansion, culminating in the Battle of Kosovo
Timur's consolidation of power in Samarkand led to his campaigns of conquest across Central Asia and the Middle East
Continuation of the Hundred Years' War

era_context: The late 14th century was marked by the consolidation of the Ming Dynasty in China, Ottoman expansion in the Balkans, the Hundred Years' War in Europe, and the rise of Timur in Central Asia. The papacy had recently returned to Rome from Avignon, and religious reform movements were gaining momentum. This was a period of significant political realignment and cultural flourishing in multiple regions.

events.title: Battle of Pontvallain
events.description: English forces under Robert Knolles defeated a French army at Pontvallain in Brittany during the Hundred Years' War. This victory demonstrated continued English military effectiveness, though it did not reverse the broader trend of English decline in France. The battle was part of the ongoing struggle for control of Brittany and the western French territories.
key_figures: French forces (commanders not clearly identified in sources)
events.certainty_note: The battle is well-attested in contemporary chronicles, particularly Froissart's detailed account. The date 1370 is firmly established.

events.title: Pope Gregory XI returns papacy to Rome
events.description: Pope Gregory XI formally returned the papal seat to Rome from Avignon in January 1377 (note: some sources date this to 1370 in terms of the decision or preparation). This move ended the Avignon Papacy (1309–1377) and was intended to restore papal authority in Rome and Italy. However, Gregory's death in 1378 and the subsequent disputed papal election led directly to the Western Schism.
key_figures: Catherine of Siena (mystic and papal advisor)

sources.name: Catherine of Siena's letters and later biographies

events.certainty_note: The return of the papacy to Rome is well-documented, though the exact chronology (1370 vs. 1377) depends on whether one dates the decision or the physical return. The year 1370 marks Gregory XI's election and the beginning of his papacy.

events.title: Ming Dynasty consolidates control over China
events.description: The Ming Dynasty, established in 1368, was consolidating its control over China by 1370. The Yongle Emperor (r. 1402–1424) was not yet in power, but the early Ming rulers were establishing administrative systems, rebuilding infrastructure damaged by the Mongol Yuan Dynasty, and beginning the cultural and artistic flourishing that would characterize the Ming period. This was a period of significant institutional reform and cultural renewal.

sources.name: Ming official records and imperial chronicles

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of Ming power is well-documented in official records. The year 1370 is clearly attested as part of this broader process.

events.title: Timur defeats Tokhtamysh and consolidates Central Asian power
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane) was engaged in military campaigns to consolidate his control over Central Asia and defeat rival khans, including Tokhtamysh. By 1370, Timur had established himself as the dominant power in the region and was beginning to expand his empire. These campaigns laid the groundwork for his later conquests across the Middle East, Persia, and into Asia Minor.
key_figures: Tokhtamysh (rival khan of the Golden Horde)
events.certainty_note: Timur's campaigns in the late 1360s–early 1370s are attested in later sources, but the exact chronology is uncertain. The year 1370 is an approximation for his consolidation of power.

events.title: Ottoman Empire expands in the Balkans
events.description: The Ottoman Empire was expanding its territorial control in the Balkans during the 1370s. Ottoman forces were consolidating their hold over territories in modern-day Bulgaria, Greece, and Serbia. This expansion was facilitated by the fragmentation of Serbian and Bulgarian states and the decline of Byzantine power. The period 1370–1371 saw the Ottoman victories that would establish them as the dominant power in the region.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans is well-attested, though specific dates for individual campaigns vary by source. The year 1370 is a reasonable approximation for the period of expansion.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Pope Gregory XI's return to Rome is sometimes given as 1377 rather than 1370, as the actual physical return occurred in January 1377. The year 1370 marks his election and the beginning of his papacy, not the return itself. Timur's exact military campaigns and their chronology are disputed in modern scholarship, with some sources placing key victories in different years. The significance of the Battle of Pontvallain has been somewhat overstated in older historiography; while it was a notable English victory, it did not significantly alter the overall trajectory of the Hundred Years' War.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1370 is moderately well-documented for Europe and China, with detailed chronicles and administrative records. Ottoman and Central Asian history relies more heavily on later compilations, creating some chronological uncertainty. The sources that survive are often shaped by court patronage and the interests of literate elites. African, American, and Oceanic history for this year is almost entirely absent from surviving written records. The Eurocentric bias in historical preservation is particularly evident for this period.

Ming consolidation of power enabled later maritime exploration
