era_context: The early 14th century was a period of profound transition across Eurasia. The Mongol Empire had fragmented into competing khanates, each struggling to maintain control over vast territories. The Yuan Dynasty in China was facing mounting internal crises. Ottoman expansion in Anatolia was accelerating. In Europe, feudal instability and dynastic conflicts were common, with the Hundred Years' War approaching. Islamic civilisation remained culturally vibrant despite political fragmentation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern and Eastern Europe beyond major kingdoms
South America and Mesoamerica (limited written records)

events.title: Succession of Philip VI of Valois — French throne dispute
events.description: Philip VI of Valois ascended to the French throne in 1328 following the death of Charles IV, the last direct Capetian king. By 1329, Philip VI had consolidated his position and was establishing himself as a legitimate ruler. However, Edward III of England (grandson of Philip IV through his mother) also had a claim to the French throne, which he would assert in 1337, leading to the Hundred Years' War. In 1329, Philip VI was still consolidating his authority and had not yet faced serious challenge to his rule. The succession of Philip VI established the Valois dynasty and set the stage for one of medieval Europe's longest and most significant conflicts.
key_figures: Philip VI of Valois (King of France, r. 1328–1350)
Charles IV (previous King of France, d. 1328)
Edward III (King of England, potential claimant)

sources.name: Grandes Chroniques de France (French royal chronicle)

events.certainty_note: Philip VI's succession in 1328 and his consolidation of power by 1329 are well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Ottoman expansion in Bithynia — Conquest of Inegöl
events.description: Ottoman forces under Orhan I captured the fortress town of Inegöl (also known as Inegöl or Inoğlu) in Bithynia, consolidating Ottoman control over the region. Inegöl was a strategically important town that controlled trade routes and agricultural lands. The capture of Inegöl was part of Orhan I's systematic conquest of Byzantine territories in northwestern Anatolia. The town became an important Ottoman administrative and economic centre. The conquest demonstrated the continued military superiority of Ottoman forces and the weakness of Byzantine garrisons in the region.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman conquest of Inegöl is traditionally dated to 1329, but Ottoman chronicles are often imprecise about dating. The conquest likely occurred within ±2–3 years of this date.
cross_references: Part of Ottoman expansion in Anatolia (1320s–1360s)

events.title: Reign of David II of Scotland — Minority and regency
events.description: David II (r. 1329–1371) ascended to the Scottish throne in 1329 as a young child following the death of his father Robert I (Robert the Bruce). David II's reign was marked by a long minority during which Scotland was governed by regents and faced continued conflict with England. In 1329, David II was crowned king, but real power lay with the regency council. His reign would be characterised by military conflicts with England, including the Battle of Halidon Hill (1331), and periods of captivity. David II's reign represented a continuation of Scottish independence struggles and the transition from the era of Robert the Bruce to a new generation of Scottish leadership.
events.certainty_note: David II's accession in 1329 is well-documented in Scottish and English sources.
cross_references: Part of Wars of Scottish Independence (1296–1357)

events.title: Death of Robert I (Robert the Bruce) — End of an era in Scotland
events.description: Robert I, known as Robert the Bruce, died on 7 June 1329 at Cardross Castle in Scotland. Robert the Bruce had been the dominant figure in Scottish history for over two decades, leading Scotland to independence from English rule through military victories (notably at Bannockburn in 1314) and diplomatic negotiations. His death marked the end of an era and the transition to a new generation of Scottish leadership under his young son David II. Robert's legacy was the establishment of Scottish independence and the Bruce dynasty. His death also removed a powerful figure who had maintained Scottish unity and military strength, leaving Scotland vulnerable to renewed English pressure during David II's minority.
key_figures: Robert I (Robert the Bruce, King of Scotland, r. 1306–1329)
events.certainty_note: Robert the Bruce's death on 7 June 1329 is well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.
cross_references: Part of Wars of Scottish Independence (1296–1357)

events.title: Yuan Dynasty administrative crisis — Factional conflicts
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty under Emperor Taiyuan (r. 1328–1329) and his successor Wenzong (r. 1328–1332) experienced severe administrative and factional crises in 1329. The Mongol ruling elite was divided into competing factions, each seeking to control the imperial court and government. These factional conflicts weakened central authority and contributed to the broader decline of Yuan rule. In 1329, the Yuan government was struggling to maintain control over vast territories and to manage the economic and social crises caused by famines and epidemics. The administrative instability of this period is well-documented in Yuan official records and later Chinese histories.
key_figures: Bayan of the Merkits (powerful Mongol official)

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Yuan Dynasty factional conflicts in 1329 are well-documented in Yuan records.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Robert the Bruce's death in 1329 has been somewhat revised by modern scholarship. While traditionally presented as a watershed moment marking the end of Scottish independence struggles, recent historians note that Scottish independence was not fully secured until the Treaty of Edinburgh-Northampton (1328) and subsequent treaties. Robert's death did not immediately threaten Scottish independence, though it did remove a powerful leader during a vulnerable period. Additionally, some sources suggest that Robert the Bruce's health had been declining for several years before his death, and his influence on governance had already diminished.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1329 is moderate and uneven. European sources (particularly Scottish and English chronicles) are relatively abundant for political and military events. Chinese sources are well-preserved but often compiled centuries later. Ottoman sources are later compilations with uncertain dating. Sub-Saharan Africa, Oceania, Central Asia, and much of the Americas remain essentially undocumented for this specific year. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military history; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented.

era_context: The early 14th century was a period of profound transition and crisis across Eurasia. The Mongol Empire had fragmented into competing khanates. The Yuan Dynasty in China was facing mounting internal pressures from famines, epidemics, and factional conflicts. Ottoman expansion in Anatolia was accelerating, threatening Byzantine territorial control. In Europe, the late medieval period was characterised by feudal instability, dynastic conflicts, and the approach of the Hundred Years' War. Islamic civilisation remained culturally vibrant despite political fragmentation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern and Eastern Europe beyond major kingdoms

events.title: Death of Charles IV of France — End of Capetian dynasty
events.description: Charles IV, the last direct Capetian king of France, died on 1 February 1328. Charles IV had reigned since 1322 and had no surviving male heir. His death marked the end of the direct Capetian line that had ruled France since 987. The succession was disputed: Philip VI of Valois (a cousin of Charles IV) claimed the throne, as did Edward III of England (grandson of Philip IV through his mother). The French nobility supported Philip VI's claim, establishing the Valois dynasty. Edward III's claim would not be seriously pressed until 1337, when he would declare himself king of France, leading to the Hundred Years' War. Charles IV's death was thus a pivotal moment in medieval European history, setting in motion events that would shape the continent for the next century.

sources.name: Grandes Chroniques de France (French royal chronicle)

events.certainty_note: Charles IV's death on 1 February 1328 is well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Accession of Philip VI of Valois — Establishment of Valois dynasty
events.description: Following Charles IV's death, Philip VI of Valois was crowned king of France in 1328. Philip VI (r. 1328–1350) was the son of Charles of Valois (brother of Philip IV) and thus a cousin of the deceased Charles IV. The French nobility supported Philip VI's claim over that of Edward III of England, establishing the principle of male succession through the male line (Salic Law). Philip VI's accession established the Valois dynasty, which would rule France for over two centuries. In 1328, Philip VI was consolidating his authority and establishing himself as a legitimate ruler. His reign would be marked by the outbreak of the Hundred Years' War in 1337 and by attempts to strengthen royal authority and expand French territorial control.
key_figures: Philip VI of Valois (King of France, r. 1328–1350)

sources.name: French royal records and coronation documents

events.certainty_note: Philip VI's accession and coronation in 1328 are well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Battle of Cassel — Flemish rebellion against French authority
events.description: French forces under Philip VI defeated a Flemish rebellion at the Battle of Cassel on 23 August 1328. The Flemish had rebelled against French authority and were led by Nicholas Zannekin, a popular leader. The French victory at Cassel reasserted French control over Flanders and demonstrated Philip VI's military strength early in his reign. The battle resulted in heavy casualties among the Flemish forces and the suppression of the rebellion. Cassel was an important victory for Philip VI, establishing his authority and military credibility. The battle also demonstrated the continued French dominance in the Low Countries and the weakness of Flemish resistance to French authority.
key_figures: Philip VI of Valois (King of France)
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Cassel on 23 August 1328 is well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The date and outcome are secure.
cross_references: Related to 1328-002 (Philip VI's consolidation of power)

events.title: Reign of Taiyuan (Emperor of Yuan) — Brief and unstable rule
events.description: Taiyuan (also known as Mingzong) reigned as Emperor of the Yuan Dynasty from 1328 to 1329, a period of only about one year. His reign was marked by factional conflicts within the Mongol ruling elite and administrative instability. Taiyuan's brief reign exemplified the broader crisis of Yuan governance in the early 14th century, as competing Mongol factions struggled for control of the imperial court. His reign was followed by that of Toghon Temür (Wenzong), who ruled from 1328 to 1332 (with some overlap and confusion in dating). The instability of Yuan imperial succession in 1328–1329 reflected the deeper problems of Mongol rule in China and contributed to the eventual collapse of the Yuan Dynasty.
key_figures: Bayan of the Merkits (powerful Mongol official)

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Yuan imperial succession in 1328–1329 is documented in Yuan records, but the precise dating and duration of individual reigns is uncertain due to overlapping claims and factional conflicts. Dating is uncertain by ±1–2 years.

events.title: Ottoman expansion in Anatolia — Consolidation of territorial gains
events.description: In 1328, Ottoman forces under Orhan I continued their systematic conquest of Byzantine territories in Anatolia. The Ottomans were consolidating their control over Bithynia and expanding into adjacent regions. The Ottoman expansion of this period was characterised by the capture of fortified towns and the displacement of Byzantine garrisons. By 1328, the Ottomans had established themselves as a major regional power and were beginning to threaten Byzantine control of the eastern Mediterranean. The Ottoman expansion of the 1320s–1330s laid the foundation for Ottoman dominance of the region and the eventual conquest of Constantinople in 1453.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in 1328 is attested in later chronicles, but precise dating of individual campaigns is uncertain. The expansion likely occurred within ±2–3 years of this date.
cross_references: Part of Ottoman expansion in Anatolia (1320s–1360s)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Battle of Cassel has been somewhat revised by modern scholarship. While traditionally presented as a decisive French victory that secured Philip VI's authority, recent historians note that the battle's long-term strategic impact was limited. Flemish resistance to French authority continued, and the region remained unstable. Additionally, the casualty figures reported in contemporary chronicles (often cited as 16,000 Flemish dead) are likely exaggerated. The battle is better understood as one episode in a prolonged Franco-Flemish conflict rather than a definitive resolution.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1328 is moderate and uneven. European sources (particularly French chronicles) are relatively abundant for political and military events. Chinese sources are well-preserved but often compiled centuries later. Ottoman sources are later compilations with uncertain dating. Sub-Saharan Africa, Oceania, Central Asia, and much of the Americas remain essentially undocumented for this specific year. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military history; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. The year 1328 is a pivotal moment in European history (the succession crisis in France), but this significance is largely a retrospective judgment based on the Hundred Years' War that followed.

events.title: Accession of Edward III of England — Beginning of a transformative reign
events.description: Edward III ascended to the English throne on 25 January 1327 following the deposition and death of his father Edward II. Edward II had been an unpopular and ineffective king, and his deposition was orchestrated by his wife Isabella of France and her lover Roger Mortimer. Edward III was only fourteen years old at his accession, and real power initially lay with Isabella and Mortimer. However, Edward III would eventually consolidate power and become one of England's most significant medieval monarchs. His reign (1327–1377) would be marked by military campaigns in Scotland, the outbreak of the Hundred Years' War with France (1337), and the development of English military tactics and institutions. Edward III's accession marked the beginning of a new era in English history.
key_figures: Roger Mortimer (regent and lover of Isabella)
events.certainty_note: Edward III's accession on 25 January 1327 is well-documented in contemporary English records. The date is secure.
cross_references: Leads to 1330-003 (Philip VI's reign and tensions with England)

events.title: Death of Edward II of England — End of a troubled reign
events.description: Edward II, King of England, died on 21 September 1327 (or possibly 1328, sources vary) at Berkeley Castle in Gloucestershire. Edward II had reigned since 1307 but had been an unpopular and ineffective king. His reign was marked by military failures in Scotland (notably the loss of the Battle of Bannockburn in 1314), conflicts with the English nobility, and favouritism toward unpopular courtiers. Edward II was deposed in January 1327 by his wife Isabella and her lover Roger Mortimer, and he died in captivity shortly thereafter. The circumstances of his death are unclear; contemporary sources suggest he was murdered, though this has been disputed by modern historians. Edward II's death marked the end of a troubled reign and the beginning of Edward III's transformative rule.
events.certainty_note: Edward II's death in 1327 is well-attested, but the exact date is uncertain. Some sources place it in 1328. The circumstances of his death (murder vs. natural causes) are disputed by modern historians.

events.title: Deposition of Edward II — Political crisis in England
events.description: Edward II was deposed as King of England on 25 January 1327 by a coalition of English nobles, clergy, and his wife Isabella of France. The deposition was orchestrated by Isabella and her lover Roger Mortimer, who had invaded England from France with military support. Edward II's deposition was justified by his military failures, his conflicts with the nobility, and his alleged incompetence. The deposition was formally ratified by Parliament, establishing a precedent for parliamentary involvement in royal succession. Edward II was forced to abdicate in favour of his young son Edward III. The deposition of Edward II was a significant moment in English constitutional history and marked the beginning of a new era under Edward III.

sources.name: English royal records and parliamentary records

events.certainty_note: Edward II's deposition on 25 January 1327 is well-documented in contemporary English records and parliamentary documents.
cross_references: Related to 1327-001 (Edward III's accession) and 1327-002 (Edward II's death)

events.title: Ottoman expansion in Anatolia — Capture of Bursa (traditional date)
events.description: Ottoman forces under Orhan I captured the Byzantine city of Bursa in 1326 or 1327 (sources vary, with some placing the capture as late as 1330). Bursa was one of the most important Byzantine cities in Anatolia and had served as a temporary capital of the Byzantine Empire. The capture of Bursa was a major strategic victory for the Ottomans, giving them control of a wealthy, well-fortified urban centre. Bursa became the first Ottoman capital and remained a major centre of Ottoman power and culture. The conquest demonstrated Ottoman military capability and marked a significant step in the decline of Byzantine territorial control in Anatolia. The exact date of Bursa's capture is disputed by modern scholars, with some arguing for 1326, others for 1327, and still others for 1330 or later.
events.certainty_note: The capture of Bursa is traditionally dated to 1326, but sources vary by ±4 years. The event itself is confirmed, but the precise year is disputed. Some modern scholars argue for 1330 or even later.
cross_references: Part of Ottoman expansion in Anatolia (1320s–1360s)

events.title: Yuan Dynasty crisis — Continued famines and epidemics
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty continued to experience severe famines, epidemics, and social unrest in 1327. The combination of natural disasters, administrative corruption, and Mongol factional conflicts created widespread suffering and fuelled peasant rebellions. These conditions are documented in Yuan official records and later Chinese histories. The famines and epidemics of the late 1320s are among the earliest documented outbreaks of plague in China and may represent the initial stages of the Black Death pandemic. The social and demographic crisis of this period contributed significantly to the eventual collapse of Yuan rule.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Famines and epidemics in Yuan China in 1327 are documented in Yuan records.
cross_references: Part of Black Death pandemic origins

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Edward II's deposition has been somewhat revised by modern scholarship. While traditionally presented as a necessary removal of an incompetent king, recent historians note that Edward II's failures were partly due to circumstances beyond his control (the strength of Scottish resistance, the power of the English nobility) and that his deposition set a dangerous precedent for future royal instability. Additionally, the role of Isabella and Mortimer in the deposition has been reassessed; Isabella is no longer universally condemned as a scheming adulteress, and her actions are now understood in the context of political necessity and her own vulnerability as a foreign queen. The dating of Bursa's capture remains disputed; while 1326 is the traditional date, modern scholars argue for 1327, 1330, or even later, reflecting the unreliability of Ottoman chronicles for precise dating.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1327 is moderate and uneven. English sources are relatively abundant for political and military events, particularly regarding Edward II's deposition and Edward III's accession. French sources are less detailed about English affairs. Chinese sources are well-preserved but often compiled centuries later. Ottoman sources are later compilations with uncertain dating. Sub-Saharan Africa, Oceania, Central Asia, and much of the Americas remain essentially undocumented for this specific year. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military history; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. The year 1327 is significant in English history (Edward III's accession), but this significance is partly retrospective, based on his later achievements.

era_context: The early 14th century CE spans the late Medieval period in Europe, the height of the Delhi Sultanate in South Asia, the Yuan Dynasty in East Asia, and the early Ottoman Empire in Anatolia. This is an era of significant political fragmentation in Europe (competing kingdoms and city-states), Islamic expansion in the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean, and Mongol-descended dynasties consolidating power across Asia. The Black Death (1347–1353) has not yet arrived in Europe, but trade networks connecting Eurasia are at peak intensity.

Oceania and Pacific islands (oral traditions not well preserved in written form)
Indigenous Americas (no written records in most regions; Inca Empire not yet dominant)

events.title: Osman I's death and succession of Orhan I
events.description: Osman I, founder of the Ottoman Empire, died in 1323 or 1324 (sources vary); his son Orhan I succeeded him and consolidated Ottoman power in northwestern Anatolia. Orhan expanded the nascent empire through conquest of Byzantine territories and reorganized the military and administrative structure. This succession marked the transition from a small beylik (principality) to a major regional power that would eventually become a world empire. The exact year of Osman's death is disputed, but Orhan's reign (1323/1326–1362) is well-attested.
events.certainty_note: Orhan's accession is well-documented in Ottoman and Byzantine sources, but the exact year of Osman's death (1323 or 1324) creates ambiguity about whether 1326 is the correct date for Orhan's formal succession. Most modern scholarship places Osman's death in 1323–1324 and Orhan's reign from 1323/1326 onward.

events.title: Isabella of France arrives in England to support her son Edward III
events.description: Queen Isabella of France, wife of Edward II of England, arrived in England in 1326 with her son Edward (later Edward III) and her lover Roger Mortimer. She had fled France after her husband Edward II rejected her, and she returned to England with military support to challenge Edward II's rule. This invasion led to Edward II's deposition and the rise of Edward III, fundamentally altering English politics and contributing to the later Hundred Years' War between England and France.
events.certainty_note: Isabella's invasion of England in 1326 is extensively documented in contemporary English and French chronicles. The date is secure.

events.title: Mansa Musa's pilgrimage to Mecca
events.region: Mali Empire, West Africa; Egypt; Arabia
events.description: Mansa Musa, ruler of the Mali Empire, departed on his famous pilgrimage (hajj) to Mecca in 1324, passing through Egypt in 1324 and reaching Mecca in 1324. The pilgrimage is often dated to 1324, though some sources place the departure in 1323 or 1324 and the return in 1325. This pilgrimage is legendary for the vast wealth displayed—Musa brought enormous quantities of gold, which reportedly caused inflation in Egypt. The pilgrimage enhanced Mali's prestige in the Islamic world and led to increased contact between Mali and the Mediterranean/Middle Eastern regions. The exact chronology is debated among scholars.
events.certainty_note: The pilgrimage occurred in 1323–1325; most modern scholarship places the departure in 1323 or 1324 and the passage through Egypt in 1324. The exact year assigned here (1326) is less certain; the pilgrimage may have been completed by this date or still in progress. Dating varies by ±1–2 years across sources.

events.title: Lucca's conflict with Florence and Pisa
events.description: In 1326, the city of Lucca, under the rule of Castruccio Castracani, engaged in military campaigns against neighboring Florence and Pisa as part of ongoing Guelph-Ghibelline factional conflicts. Castruccio was a prominent Ghibelline leader who sought to expand Lucca's power in Tuscany. These conflicts were part of the broader Italian political fragmentation of the 14th century, where city-states competed for regional dominance.

sources.name: Giovanni Villani, Nuova Cronica (14th century, near-contemporary)

events.certainty_note: Castruccio's military activities in the mid-1320s are well-documented, though the specific events of 1326 are less precisely dated in surviving sources. The year is assigned by historiographic convention based on chronicle accounts.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Mansa Musa's pilgrimage is contested: some sources place it in 1323, others in 1324, and a few in 1325. The year 1326 is not the primary date assigned by most modern scholars, who favor 1324 for the passage through Egypt. The exact year of Osman I's death (and thus Orhan I's accession) is also disputed between 1323 and 1324, making the assignment to 1326 uncertain. Isabella of France's invasion is well-dated to 1326, but the political consequences (Edward II's deposition) occurred in 1327, not 1326.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1326 is uneven. European events (Isabella's invasion, Italian city-state conflicts) are well-documented in contemporary chronicles and administrative records. Ottoman and Islamic sources are more distant from the events (mostly later compilations), creating dating uncertainty. West African history relies heavily on Islamic geographers and travelers (al-Umari, Ibn Battuta) whose accounts are sometimes vague about precise chronology. The record is heavily weighted toward Mediterranean and European events; East Asian, South Asian, and sub-Saharan African events are underrepresented in surviving sources accessible to modern scholarship.

graph_edges.note: Isabella's invasion in 1326 directly led to Edward II's deposition in 1327
Orhan I's succession in 1326 began his reign, which lasted until 1362

era_context: The early 14th century CE continues with the same major powers: the Delhi Sultanate, Yuan Dynasty, Ottoman Empire, and fragmented European kingdoms. The Mali Empire is at its height under Mansa Musa. This is the era immediately before the Black Death transforms Eurasia. Trade networks, particularly the Silk Road and Indian Ocean routes, are active and prosperous.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia (limited written records from this period)

events.title: Mansa Musa's pilgrimage to Mecca (continued/return journey)
events.region: Mali Empire, West Africa; Egypt; Arabia
events.description: Mansa Musa's pilgrimage to Mecca, which began in 1323 or 1324, continued through 1325. Some sources place his return journey through Egypt in 1325. The pilgrimage is one of the most famous in Islamic history, notable for the enormous wealth displayed and the impact on Egyptian economy and Mali's international prestige. The exact chronology remains debated among scholars.
events.certainty_note: The pilgrimage spanned 1323–1325 or 1324–1325 depending on the source. The exact year of return is uncertain; 1325 is a plausible date for the return journey, but some sources suggest 1324.

events.title: Aztec founding of Tenochtitlan
events.description: According to Aztec tradition, the Mexica people founded the city of Tenochtitlan on an island in Lake Texcoco in 1325 CE. This date is recorded in Aztec codices and later Spanish colonial accounts. The founding followed a migration from Aztlán and was marked by the discovery of an eagle perched on a cactus, which the Mexica interpreted as a divine sign. Tenochtitlan would become one of the largest cities in the world by the 16th century. However, modern archaeological evidence suggests the site may have been occupied earlier, and the traditional date of 1325 may be symbolic or approximate.

sources.name: Codex Mendoza (16th century, post-conquest Aztec codex)
Bernal Díaz del Castillo, The True History of the Conquest of New Spain (16th century)
Archaeological evidence from Mexico City excavations

events.certainty_note: The date 1325 comes from Aztec tradition recorded in codices and Spanish colonial sources. Modern archaeology suggests occupation of the site may have begun earlier (possibly 14th century, but not necessarily 1325). The traditional date is culturally significant but historically uncertain. Archaeological stratigraphy does not precisely confirm 1325.

events.title: Edward II's reign continues; political instability in England
events.description: Edward II of England continued his troubled reign in 1325, marked by conflicts with the nobility, military failures in Scotland, and personal scandals involving his favorites (particularly Piers Gaveston and later Hugh Despenser). The year 1325 saw ongoing baronial opposition to Edward II's rule. This instability would culminate in Isabella's invasion in 1326 and Edward II's deposition in 1327.
events.certainty_note: Edward II's reign and the political conflicts of 1325 are extensively documented in contemporary English records and chronicles.

events.title: Delhi Sultanate under Muhammad bin Tughluq
events.description: Muhammad bin Tughluq (r. 1325–1351) ascended to the throne of the Delhi Sultanate in 1325, succeeding his father Ghazi ud-Din Tughluq. Muhammad bin Tughluq is remembered as an ambitious but controversial ruler who attempted major administrative and monetary reforms. His reign saw significant territorial expansion but also economic disruption and rebellion. The early years of his reign (1325–1330) were marked by consolidation of power and ambitious projects.
key_figures: Muhammad bin Tughluq (Sultan of Delhi, r. 1325–1351)

sources.name: Ziauddin Barani, Tarikh-i Firoz Shahi (14th century, near-contemporary)

events.certainty_note: Muhammad bin Tughluq's accession in 1325 is well-documented in contemporary Islamic chronicles and court records.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Tenochtitlan's founding (1325) is traditional and symbolic rather than archaeologically confirmed. Modern excavations have not definitively established 1325 as the founding date; the site may have been occupied earlier or the date may be approximate. The traditional Aztec date should be treated as legendary or approximate rather than confirmed historical fact. Regarding Mansa Musa's pilgrimage, the exact year of return (1325 vs. 1324) is disputed among sources.
historiographic_note: The record for 1325 is heavily weighted toward Islamic and European sources. English political history is well-documented through royal records and chronicles. Islamic sources (Ibn Battuta, Barani) provide detailed accounts of the Delhi Sultanate and Mali Empire, though with some chronological ambiguity. Mesoamerican history relies entirely on post-conquest Aztec codices and Spanish colonial accounts, making the dating of Tenochtitlan's founding uncertain. Sub-Saharan Africa, East Asia, and indigenous Americas are underrepresented in the surviving written record.

graph_edges.note: Muhammad bin Tughluq's reign lasted from 1325 to 1351
Political instability in England in 1325 led to Isabella's invasion in 1326

era_context: The early 14th century CE continues with the Delhi Sultanate, Yuan Dynasty, Ottoman Empire, and Mali Empire at their heights. This is the era of the Hundred Years' War's origins (Anglo-French tensions), the height of Islamic scholarship and travel, and the consolidation of Mongol-descended dynasties across Asia. The Black Death has not yet arrived in Europe.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and the Steppe (limited written records)

events.title: Mansa Musa's pilgrimage to Mecca (passage through Egypt)
events.region: Mali Empire, West Africa; Egypt; Arabia
events.description: Mansa Musa, emperor of the Mali Empire, passed through Egypt in 1324 on his pilgrimage to Mecca. His arrival in Cairo with an enormous retinue and vast quantities of gold is extensively documented in Egyptian and Islamic sources. The pilgrimage is famous for the wealth displayed—contemporary accounts suggest Musa brought 60,000 people and 80 camels carrying gold dust. The influx of gold reportedly caused inflation in Egypt. This pilgrimage enhanced Mali's prestige in the Islamic world and established direct contact between Mali and the Mediterranean/Middle Eastern regions.
events.certainty_note: Mansa Musa's passage through Egypt in 1324 is extensively documented in contemporary Egyptian and Islamic sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Osman I's death (disputed date)
events.description: Osman I, founder of the Ottoman Empire, died in 1323 or 1324 (sources vary). His death marked the end of the founding generation of the Ottoman state and the succession of his son Orhan I. Osman had transformed a small beylik into a significant regional power through military conquest and administrative organization. The exact year of his death is disputed among Ottoman chronicles, with some sources placing it in 1323 and others in 1324.
key_figures: Osman I (Founder of Ottoman Empire, d. 1323/1324)
events.certainty_note: Osman I's death is dated to either 1323 or 1324 in different sources. Ottoman chronicles, written in the 15th century, are not contemporary and contain some chronological ambiguity. Byzantine sources are more contemporary but less detailed about Ottoman internal succession.

events.title: Council of Avignon convened by Pope John XXII
events.description: Pope John XXII (r. 1316–1334) convened ecclesiastical councils and issued papal bulls in 1324 addressing theological disputes, particularly regarding Franciscan poverty and the nature of papal authority. The year 1324 saw significant papal activity in defining Church doctrine and addressing heresy. John XXII was a controversial pope who engaged in disputes with the Holy Roman Emperor and Franciscan theologians.
key_figures: William of Ockham (Franciscan theologian, opponent of John XXII)
events.certainty_note: Pope John XXII's theological activities and disputes are well-documented, though the specific events of 1324 are less precisely dated in surviving sources. The year is assigned by historiographic convention.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty under Yesün Temür (Taidian Emperor)
events.description: Yesün Temür (Taidian Emperor, r. 1323–1328) ruled the Yuan Dynasty in 1324. His reign was marked by administrative reforms and attempts to stabilize the dynasty after years of internal conflict. Yesün Temür sought to restore Confucian governance and reduce Mongol military dominance in administration. His reign was relatively stable compared to the turbulent period before his accession.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty, compiled 14th century)

events.certainty_note: Yesün Temür's reign and the date 1324 are well-documented in Yuan Dynasty records and the official Yuan Shi.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact year of Osman I's death is disputed between 1323 and 1324; assigning it to 1324 is somewhat arbitrary. Ottoman chronicles, the primary sources for this event, were written 150+ years after the fact and contain chronological ambiguities. The significance of Pope John XXII's activities in 1324 is debated; some scholars emphasize his theological contributions, while others view his papacy as marked by political overreach and controversy.
historiographic_note: The record for 1324 is uneven. Islamic sources (al-Umari, Ibn Battuta) provide detailed accounts of Mansa Musa's pilgrimage, which is one of the best-documented events of the year. European ecclesiastical and political records are also relatively rich. East Asian (Yuan Dynasty) records are well-preserved in official histories. Ottoman sources are later compilations with chronological uncertainty. Sub-Saharan Africa (beyond Mali), indigenous Americas, and Central Asia are underrepresented in surviving written records.

graph_edges.note: Mansa Musa's passage through Egypt in 1324 was followed by his return journey in 1325
Osman I's death in 1323/1324 led to Orhan I's succession (formally recognized by 1326)
Yesün Temür's reign lasted from 1323 to 1328

era_context: The early 14th century CE is marked by the height of the Delhi Sultanate under the Tughluq dynasty, the Yuan Dynasty in East Asia, the Ottoman Empire's early expansion in Anatolia, and the Mali Empire's dominance in West Africa. This is an era of active trade networks, Islamic scholarship, and political fragmentation in Europe. The Black Death has not yet arrived.

events.title: Osman I's death (primary date)
events.description: Osman I, founder of the Ottoman Empire, died in 1323 (or possibly 1324; sources vary). Osman had transformed a small beylik (principality) in northwestern Anatolia into a significant regional power through military conquest and administrative organization. His death marked the end of the founding generation and the succession of his son Orhan I, who would further expand and consolidate Ottoman power. Osman's legacy was the establishment of a dynasty that would eventually become a world empire.
key_figures: Osman I (Founder of Ottoman Empire, d. 1323/1324)
events.certainty_note: Osman I's death is dated to 1323 or 1324 in different sources. Ottoman chronicles, written 150+ years after the events, contain chronological ambiguities. Byzantine sources are more contemporary but less detailed about Ottoman succession. Modern scholarship is divided on whether 1323 or 1324 is correct.

events.title: Mansa Musa's departure on pilgrimage to Mecca
events.description: Mansa Musa, emperor of the Mali Empire, departed on his pilgrimage (hajj) to Mecca in 1323 or 1324 (sources vary on the exact year). The pilgrimage is one of the most famous in Islamic history, notable for the enormous wealth displayed and the impact on the economies of regions through which Musa traveled. The pilgrimage would take him through North Africa and Egypt, establishing direct contact between Mali and the Islamic Mediterranean world.
events.certainty_note: The pilgrimage is dated to 1323–1325 or 1324–1325 depending on the source. The exact year of departure is uncertain; 1323 and 1324 are both plausible. Modern scholarship is divided on the precise chronology.

events.title: Ghazi ud-Din Tughluq's death and Muhammad bin Tughluq's accession
events.description: Ghazi ud-Din Tughluq, Sultan of Delhi, died in 1323, and his son Muhammad bin Tughluq (also known as Muhammad of the Tughlaq dynasty) succeeded him. Muhammad bin Tughluq (r. 1325–1351, though some sources date his accession to 1323) is remembered as an ambitious but controversial ruler who attempted major administrative and monetary reforms. The exact year of his accession is disputed between 1323 and 1325 in different sources.
key_figures: Ghazi ud-Din Tughluq (Sultan of Delhi, d. 1323)

sources.name: Ziauddin Barani, Tarikh-i Firoz Shahi (14th century, near-contemporary)

events.certainty_note: Ghazi ud-Din Tughluq's death in 1323 is well-attested, but Muhammad bin Tughluq's accession is dated to either 1323 or 1325 in different sources. The discrepancy may reflect a period of transition or regency. Most modern scholarship favors 1325 as the formal accession date.

events.title: Yesün Temür's accession to Yuan throne
events.description: Yesün Temür (Taidian Emperor) ascended to the throne of the Yuan Dynasty in 1323, following a period of internal conflict and succession disputes. His accession marked a shift toward administrative stability and Confucian governance. Yesün Temür sought to reduce Mongol military dominance in administration and restore traditional Chinese bureaucratic practices.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty, compiled 14th century)

events.certainty_note: Yesün Temür's accession in 1323 is well-documented in Yuan Dynasty records and the official Yuan Shi.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact year of Osman I's death is disputed between 1323 and 1324; sources are inconsistent. Ottoman chronicles, written 150+ years after the fact, are not contemporary and contain chronological ambiguities. The date of Muhammad bin Tughluq's accession is also disputed between 1323 and 1325, with most modern scholarship favoring 1325. The year 1323 may represent a period of transition rather than a clear succession date.
historiographic_note: The record for 1323 is moderate in quality. Islamic sources (al-Umari, Ibn Battuta, Barani) provide accounts of events in the Delhi Sultanate and Mali Empire, though with some chronological ambiguity. East Asian (Yuan Dynasty) records are well-preserved in official histories. Ottoman sources are later compilations with significant dating uncertainty. European records for 1323 are sparse in this dataset. Sub-Saharan Africa, indigenous Americas, and Central Asia are underrepresented in surviving written records.

graph_edges.note: Osman I's death in 1323 led to Orhan I's succession (formally recognized by 1326)
Mansa Musa's departure in 1323 led to his passage through Egypt in 1324
Ghazi ud-Din Tughluq's death in 1323 led to Muhammad bin Tughluq's reign (formally dated 1325–1351)
Yesün Temür's reign lasted from 1323 to 1328

era_context: The early 14th century CE continues with the Delhi Sultanate, Yuan Dynasty, Ottoman Empire, and Mali Empire as major powers. This is an era of active trade networks, Islamic scholarship, and political fragmentation in Europe. The Black Death has not yet arrived in Eurasia. The Hundred Years' War between England and France is approaching.

events.title: Battle of Mühldorf (Holy Roman Empire succession dispute)
events.description: The Battle of Mühldorf was fought in 1322 between Louis IV of Bavaria and Frederick III of Austria over succession to the Holy Roman Empire. Louis IV defeated Frederick III, establishing Louis's claim to the imperial throne. This battle was part of a broader succession dispute that would dominate Holy Roman politics for years. The victory at Mühldorf was a significant step in Louis IV's path to becoming Holy Roman Emperor (crowned 1328).
key_figures: Louis IV of Bavaria (later Holy Roman Emperor)

sources.name: Contemporary German chronicles (e.g., Chronicon Henrici de Diessenhofen)

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Mühldorf in 1322 is well-documented in contemporary German chronicles and imperial records. The date is secure.
cross_references: 1328-001 (Louis IV's coronation as Holy Roman Emperor)

events.title: Castruccio Castracani's military campaigns in Tuscany
events.description: Castruccio Castracani, lord of Lucca, engaged in military campaigns in Tuscany in 1322 as part of ongoing Guelph-Ghibelline factional conflicts. Castruccio was a prominent Ghibelline leader who sought to expand Lucca's power and influence in the region. These campaigns were part of the broader Italian political fragmentation of the 14th century.

sources.name: Giovanni Villani, Nuova Cronica (14th century, near-contemporary)

events.certainty_note: Castruccio's military activities in the early 1320s are well-documented, though the specific events of 1322 are less precisely dated in surviving sources. The year is assigned by historiographic convention.

events.title: Delhi Sultanate under Ghazi ud-Din Tughluq
events.description: Ghazi ud-Din Tughluq (r. 1320–1323) ruled the Delhi Sultanate in 1322. His reign was marked by military campaigns and administrative consolidation. Ghazi ud-Din was the father of Muhammad bin Tughluq, who would succeed him in 1323 or 1325. The year 1322 falls in the middle of Ghazi ud-Din's reign, a period of relative stability in the Sultanate.
key_figures: Ghazi ud-Din Tughluq (Sultan of Delhi, r. 1320–1323)

sources.name: Ziauddin Barani, Tarikh-i Firoz Shahi (14th century, near-contemporary)

events.certainty_note: Ghazi ud-Din Tughluq's reign and the date 1322 are well-documented in Islamic chronicles.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty under Yingzong (Shidebala)
events.description: Yingzong (Shidebala), emperor of the Yuan Dynasty, ruled in 1322. His reign (1321–1323) was marked by administrative reforms and attempts to stabilize the dynasty. Yingzong sought to restore Confucian governance and reduce factional conflicts within the imperial court. His reign was relatively short, lasting only about two years before his death in 1323.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty, compiled 14th century)

events.certainty_note: Yingzong's reign and the date 1322 are well-documented in Yuan Dynasty records and the official Yuan Shi.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Battle of Mühldorf is sometimes overstated in popular accounts; while it was an important victory for Louis IV, the Holy Roman succession dispute continued for several more years before Louis's coronation in 1328. The battle did not immediately resolve the conflict. Castruccio Castracani's specific activities in 1322 are less well-documented than his later campaigns; the year may be approximate.
historiographic_note: The record for 1322 is moderate in quality. European military and political events (Battle of Mühldorf, Italian city-state conflicts) are reasonably well-documented in contemporary chronicles and records. Islamic sources (Barani, Ibn Battuta) provide accounts of the Delhi Sultanate. East Asian (Yuan Dynasty) records are well-preserved in official histories. Sub-Saharan Africa, indigenous Americas, and Central Asia are underrepresented in surviving written records. The overall documentation is weighted toward Mediterranean and European events.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Mühldorf in 1322 was a step toward Louis IV's coronation as Holy Roman Emperor in 1328
Ghazi ud-Din Tughluq's reign in 1322 ended with his death in 1323
Yingzong's reign in 1322 ended with his death in 1323 and Yesün Temür's accession

era_context: The early 14th century saw the fragmentation of Mongol unity following Kublai Khan's death (1294). The Yuan Dynasty ruled China but faced increasing Han Chinese resistance. In Europe, the Avignon Papacy dominated religious politics; the Hundred Years' War between England and France was in its infancy (begun 1337, but dynastic tensions were acute). The Mali Empire dominated West Africa; the Delhi Sultanate controlled much of South Asia; the Ottoman Empire was consolidating Anatolian power.

events.title: Mansa Musa's pilgrimage preparations and Mali's zenith
events.description: Mansa Musa, ruler of the Mali Empire, was consolidating power and preparing for his famous hajj to Mecca (which occurred in 1324). By 1321, Mali was at the height of its territorial and economic power, controlling trans-Saharan gold trade routes. The empire's wealth and Islamic scholarship were flourishing, with Timbuktu emerging as a major centre of learning. This period represents the apex of Mali's influence before internal fragmentation in the late 14th century.

sources.name: Ibn Battuta, Rihla (travel account, written c. 1355–1356, describing Mali in the 1320s)
Al-Umari, Masalik al-absar (written c. 1340, describing Mali's wealth and governance)

events.certainty_note: The exact year 1321 is not explicitly documented for Mali's state; this entry reflects the broader context of Mali's power in the early 1320s, confirmed by later Arab geographers and modern historiographic consensus.

events.title: Famine and social unrest in England
events.description: England experienced severe famine conditions in 1321–1322, following poor harvests and livestock disease. This period of dearth contributed to social tension and economic hardship across the realm. The famine exacerbated existing grievances among peasants and labourers, contributing to the broader context of social instability that would culminate in the Peasants' Revolt of 1381.

sources.name: Chronicles of the period (e.g., Froissart, Knighton)

events.certainty_note: The famine of 1321–1322 is well attested in English chronicles, though precise dating and regional variation are not uniformly documented.

events.title: Conflict between the Papacy and the Visconti in Italy
events.description: Tensions between Pope John XXII (ruling from Avignon) and the Visconti family of Milan intensified in 1321. The Visconti, rising as a major power in northern Italy, resisted papal authority and territorial claims. This conflict was part of the broader struggle between temporal and spiritual authority in medieval Italy, with the Visconti eventually becoming one of the dominant powers of the Italian peninsula.

sources.name: Villani, Giovanni, Nuova Cronica (Florentine chronicle, written early 14th century)

events.certainty_note: Papal-Visconti tensions are documented in contemporary papal records, though specific events in 1321 are not always precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of 1321 as a discrete historical year is limited; most major events of this period are better understood as part of longer processes (Mali's rise, English famine cycles, Italian factional conflicts). No specific disconfirming evidence for the events listed, but their individual importance should not be overstated.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1321 is uneven. European sources (English chronicles, papal records, Italian city records) are relatively abundant. African sources are sparse and mediated through later Arab geographers. Asian sources (Yuan Dynasty records, Delhi Sultanate chronicles) exist but are not equally accessible in Western scholarship. The year itself has no particular historiographic significance; it is treated here as part of broader early 14th-century trends.

graph_edges.note: Mali's consolidation under Mansa Musa in 1321 directly enabled his famous hajj in 1324.
The famine of 1321–1322 is part of the broader context of peasant grievances leading to the 1381 Peasants' Revolt.

era_context: The early 14th century continued to see the consolidation of regional powers following the fragmentation of the Mongol Empire. The Yuan Dynasty in China faced increasing internal instability. In Europe, feudal hierarchies were being tested by economic change and dynastic ambitions. The Delhi Sultanate was expanding in South Asia; the Ottoman Empire was consolidating power in Anatolia.

events.title: Declaration of Arbroath and Scottish independence
events.description: On 6 April 1320, Scottish nobles and clergy issued the Declaration of Arbroath, a letter to Pope John XXII asserting Scotland's independence from England and the legitimacy of Robert I (Robert the Bruce) as king. This document is one of the earliest statements of national sovereignty and popular consent to governance in European history. It was issued during the Wars of Scottish Independence and sought papal support against English claims. The declaration became a foundational text in Scottish national identity.
key_figures: Robert I (Robert the Bruce, King of Scotland)

sources.name: Declaration of Arbroath (original document, preserved in the National Records of Scotland)
Barbour, John, The Brus (Scottish epic poem, written c. 1375, describing Robert the Bruce)

events.certainty_note: The Declaration of Arbroath is dated precisely to 6 April 1320 and survives as an original document. Its authenticity and significance are beyond dispute.
cross_references: 1314-001 (Battle of Bannockburn, which preceded this declaration)

events.title: Execution of Matteo Visconti and Visconti family consolidation
events.description: Matteo Visconti, the aging patriarch of the Visconti family, was imprisoned by papal forces in 1320 but was released and continued to consolidate Visconti power in Milan. This period saw the Visconti family emerging as one of the dominant powers in northern Italy, challenging both papal authority and rival city-states. The family's rise marked a shift toward signorial rule in Italian city-states.
events.certainty_note: Matteo Visconti's imprisonment and release are documented in chronicles and papal records, though the exact chronology of 1320 is not always precise.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty instability and Mongol succession disputes
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty under Emperor Yingzong faced internal instability and succession disputes in 1320. The death of Kublai Khan (1294) had left the Mongol succession fragmented, and by 1320, the Yuan Dynasty was experiencing increasing pressure from Han Chinese resistance movements and internal Mongol factional conflicts. This period marked the beginning of the dynasty's decline, which would culminate in its fall in 1368.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) and Yuan Dynasty official records
Historiographic consensus on Yuan Dynasty decline

events.certainty_note: Yuan Dynasty instability in the early 14th century is well documented, though specific events in 1320 are not always precisely dated in surviving sources.

events.title: Delhi Sultanate expansion under Muhammad bin Tughluq's predecessors
events.description: The Delhi Sultanate, under the Tughlaq dynasty, continued to expand its territorial control in South Asia in 1320. The sultanate was consolidating power over much of northern and central India, establishing administrative systems and military infrastructure. This period preceded the reign of Muhammad bin Tughluq (r. 1325–1351), who would become one of the most ambitious and controversial sultans.
key_figures: Ghazi Malik (founder of Tughlaq dynasty, later Ghiyath al-Din Tughluq)

sources.name: Ziauddin Barani, Tarikh-i Firuz Shahi (chronicle of Delhi Sultanate, written 14th century)

events.certainty_note: The Delhi Sultanate's expansion in 1320 is part of broader Tughlaq consolidation, but specific events in this year are not precisely documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The Declaration of Arbroath is sometimes overstated in modern Scottish historiography as a uniquely democratic or nationalist document; modern scholarship emphasizes that it was a strategic papal petition by an elite, not a popular movement. Its significance for later Scottish identity is undeniable, but its immediate political impact was limited—England did not recognize Scottish independence, and the wars continued. The Visconti family's rise is sometimes portrayed as a straightforward consolidation of power, but it involved complex factional struggles and papal opposition that are not fully resolved in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1320 is better than 1321, particularly for Scotland and Italy, where primary sources (the Declaration, papal records, city chronicles) are abundant. Asian sources (Yuan, Delhi) are less accessible in Western scholarship and rely heavily on later compilations. African sources remain sparse. The year 1320 is historically significant primarily for the Declaration of Arbroath; other events are part of longer processes.

graph_edges.note: The Declaration of Arbroath was issued following Robert the Bruce's victory at Bannockburn (1314), which secured Scottish military independence.
Yuan Dynasty instability in 1320 was part of the process leading to the dynasty's fall in 1368.

era_context: The early 14th century saw regional consolidation across Eurasia and Africa following the fragmentation of the Mongol Empire. In Europe, feudal structures were being tested by economic and dynastic pressures. The Mali Empire dominated West African trade; the Delhi Sultanate was consolidating power in South Asia; the Yuan Dynasty in China faced increasing instability.

events.title: Battle of Mühldorf and Bavarian succession dispute
events.description: In 1319, a succession dispute in Bavaria led to military conflict. The death of Duke Stephen III of Bavaria in 1310 had left the duchy contested between the Wittelsbach and Habsburg families. By 1319, tensions were high, and military skirmishes occurred. The conflict would culminate in the Battle of Mühldorf in 1322, one of the largest medieval battles in the Holy Roman Empire. This dispute reflected broader tensions within the Empire over succession and territorial control.

sources.name: Contemporary German chronicles and imperial records
Historiographic consensus on Bavarian succession disputes

events.certainty_note: The Bavarian succession dispute is well documented, but specific events in 1319 are not always precisely dated; the major battle occurred in 1322.

events.title: Famine and plague in Northern Europe
events.description: Northern Europe experienced severe famine conditions in 1319–1320, following poor harvests and livestock disease. This famine, sometimes called the 'Great Famine,' affected England, Scandinavia, and the Low Countries. The famine caused significant mortality and social disruption, contributing to economic hardship and social tension. It is sometimes seen as a precursor to the Black Death (1347–1353), though the connection is debated.
events.certainty_note: The Great Famine of 1319–1320 is well attested in chronicles, though precise dating and regional variation are not uniformly documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The connection between the Great Famine of 1319–1320 and the Black Death is sometimes overstated; modern scholarship suggests the famine weakened populations but was not a direct cause of plague transmission. The Bavarian succession dispute is sometimes portrayed as a straightforward conflict between two families, but it involved complex imperial politics and multiple claimants that are not fully resolved in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1319 is sparse. European sources (chronicles, imperial records) provide some information, but specific events in this year are often not precisely dated. Asian and African sources are largely absent from Western scholarship for this year. The year 1319 has no particular historiographic significance and is treated here as part of broader early 14th-century trends.

graph_edges.note: The Great Famine of 1319–1320 is sometimes seen as part of the broader context of 14th-century demographic crisis, though the connection to the Black Death is debated.
The Bavarian succession dispute of 1319 escalated to the Battle of Mühldorf in 1322.

era_context: The early 14th century saw the consolidation of regional powers following the fragmentation of the Mongol Empire. In Europe, feudal hierarchies were being tested by economic change and dynastic ambitions. The Mali Empire dominated West African trade; the Delhi Sultanate was consolidating power in South Asia; the Yuan Dynasty in China faced increasing internal instability.

events.title: Papal election of Pope John XXII
events.description: Pope John XXII (Jacques d'Euse) was elected pope in 1316 and was firmly established in Avignon by 1318. His papacy (1316–1334) was marked by administrative reforms, theological controversies (particularly regarding poverty and the nature of the Beatific Vision), and conflicts with secular rulers. John XXII was one of the most influential popes of the 14th century, and his papacy represented the consolidation of the Avignon Papacy, which had moved from Rome in 1309.
events.certainty_note: Pope John XXII's election and papacy are well documented in papal records and contemporary chronicles.

events.title: Continued Mongol fragmentation and regional consolidation
events.region: Mongol successor states (Yuan, Chagatai, Golden Horde, Ilkhanate)
events.description: By 1318, the fragmentation of the Mongol Empire into regional successor states was well established. The Yuan Dynasty in China, the Chagatai Khanate in Central Asia, the Golden Horde in Eastern Europe, and the Ilkhanate in Persia were all functioning as independent powers. This period marked the consolidation of these regional states and the beginning of their decline as Mongol unity dissolved. The process would continue throughout the 14th century.
key_figures: Mongol khans of various successor states

sources.name: Yuan Dynasty records, Ilkhanate chronicles, and Russian sources

events.certainty_note: The fragmentation of the Mongol Empire is well documented, though specific events in 1318 are not always precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: Pope John XXII is sometimes portrayed as a purely administrative figure, but modern scholarship emphasizes his significant theological influence and his controversial positions on poverty and the Beatific Vision, which generated substantial opposition. The Mongol fragmentation is sometimes presented as a simple decline, but modern scholarship recognizes that the successor states were often more administratively sophisticated and stable than the unified empire.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1318 is sparse. European sources (papal records, chronicles) provide some information, but specific events in this year are often not precisely dated. Asian sources (Yuan, Ilkhanate) exist but are less accessible in Western scholarship. African sources are largely absent. The year 1318 has no particular historiographic significance and is treated here as part of broader early 14th-century trends.

graph_edges.note: Pope John XXII's establishment in Avignon in 1318 was part of the broader Avignon Papacy, which began in 1309.

era_context: The early 14th century saw the consolidation of regional powers following the fragmentation of the Mongol Empire. In Europe, feudal hierarchies were being tested by economic change and dynastic ambitions. The Mali Empire dominated West African trade; the Delhi Sultanate was consolidating power in South Asia; the Yuan Dynasty in China faced increasing internal instability.

events.title: Famine and economic crisis in Northern Europe
events.description: Northern Europe, particularly England and Scandinavia, experienced severe famine conditions beginning in 1317, following poor harvests and livestock disease. This famine, sometimes called the 'Great Famine,' would persist through 1319–1320 and caused significant mortality and social disruption. The famine contributed to economic hardship and social tension across the region.
events.certainty_note: The Great Famine beginning in 1317 is well attested in chronicles, though precise dating and regional variation are not uniformly documented.

events.title: Robert the Bruce's consolidation of Scottish power
events.description: Robert I (Robert the Bruce), King of Scotland, continued to consolidate his power and secure Scottish independence from England in 1317. Following his victory at Bannockburn (1314), Robert the Bruce was establishing Scotland as a recognized independent kingdom. In 1317, he was strengthening his position through military campaigns and diplomatic efforts, laying the groundwork for the Declaration of Arbroath (1320).
key_figures: Robert I (Robert the Bruce, King of Scotland)

sources.name: Barbour, John, The Brus (Scottish epic poem, written c. 1375)

events.certainty_note: Robert the Bruce's consolidation of power in the years following Bannockburn (1314) is well documented, though specific events in 1317 are not always precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The Great Famine of 1317–1320 is sometimes overstated as a unique catastrophe; modern scholarship recognizes that famine was a recurring phenomenon in medieval Europe, and the 1317–1320 famine, while severe, was not unprecedented. Robert the Bruce's consolidation of power is sometimes portrayed as straightforward, but it involved complex military and diplomatic maneuvering against English opposition that is not fully resolved in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1317 is sparse. European sources (chronicles, Scottish records) provide some information, but specific events in this year are often not precisely dated. Asian and African sources are largely absent from Western scholarship for this year. The year 1317 has no particular historiographic significance and is treated here as part of broader early 14th-century trends.

graph_edges.note: The famine beginning in 1317 continued and intensified through 1319–1320.
Robert the Bruce's consolidation of power in 1317 enabled the issuance of the Declaration of Arbroath in 1320.

era_context: The early 14th century saw the fragmentation of Mongol unity following Kublai Khan's death (1294). The Yuan Dynasty under Ayurbarwada ruled China; the Ilkhanate under Öljeitü dominated Persia and the Levant; the Golden Horde controlled the Russian steppes; and the Chagatai Khanate held Central Asia. In Europe, the Capetian dynasty ruled France under Louis X; England was under Edward II; the Papacy remained at Avignon. This was a period of climatic stress—the Great Famine (1315–1317) was beginning to devastate Northern Europe.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records for this period)
Southeast Asia (sparse European documentation; local chronicles less accessible in English-language scholarship)

events.title: Great Famine of 1315–1317 intensifies across Northern Europe
events.region: Northern Europe (England, France, Low Countries, Scandinavia, German lands)
events.description: Severe crop failures caused by prolonged wet weather and cold temperatures devastated harvests across Northern Europe in 1315–1316. Grain prices soared; livestock died; malnutrition weakened populations and increased susceptibility to disease. The famine killed an estimated 5–10% of the population in affected regions and persisted into 1317. This environmental crisis destabilized feudal economies, triggered social unrest, and created conditions for the later Black Death (1347–1353) by weakening immune systems and forcing population movements.

sources.name: Jean de Venette, Chronicle (compiled 14th century, contemporary observations)
Historiographic consensus from climate and demographic studies (e.g., Benedictow, Campbell)
Manorial records and grain price data from England and France

events.certainty_note: The Great Famine is well-attested in contemporary manorial records, chronicles, and modern climate reconstructions. Dating to 1315–1317 is secure.

events.title: Death of Louis X of France; succession crisis
events.description: Louis X (Louis le Hutin, 'the Quarrelsome') died on 5 June 1316, leaving a pregnant widow, Clementia of Hungary. His death triggered a succession dispute: his brother Philip V claimed the throne, arguing that a female or unborn child could not inherit. When Clementia gave birth to a son (Jean I) in November 1316, the infant lived only five days, dying on 20 November. Philip V's claim prevailed, establishing the principle that women could not inherit the French throne—a precedent that would shape European politics for centuries and eventually contribute to the Hundred Years' War (1337–1453).
key_figures: Philip V of France (brother, claimed throne)
Jean I of France (infant son, lived 5 days)
events.certainty_note: Dates and succession are well-documented in royal records and contemporary chronicles. The death of Louis X on 5 June 1316 and Jean I's brief life (November 1316) are secure.

events.title: Philip V crowned King of France
events.description: Following the death of Louis X and the brief life of his infant son Jean I, Philip V was crowned King of France in 1316. His coronation formalized the exclusion of women from the French succession—a principle that would define Capetian and Valois succession law and later become a flashpoint in the Hundred Years' War when Edward III of England (descended from a female line) claimed the French throne.
events.certainty_note: Philip V's coronation in 1316 is well-documented in royal records and chronicles.

events.title: Öljeitü's Mausoleum (Mausoleum of Öljeitü) completed in Soltaniyeh, Ilkhanate
events.description: The Mausoleum of Öljeitü (also called the Dome of Soltaniyeh), commissioned by the Ilkhanate ruler Öljeitü (r. 1304–1316), was completed in 1316 in Soltaniyeh (modern-day northwestern Iran). The structure features an octagonal plan and a massive dome—one of the largest brick domes in the world at the time—and represents a synthesis of Persian, Byzantine, and Islamic architectural traditions. The mausoleum served as Öljeitü's burial place and was a major architectural achievement of the Ilkhanate period, demonstrating the dynasty's wealth and cultural sophistication.
events.certainty_note: The mausoleum's completion date of 1316 is supported by architectural analysis and epigraphic evidence. Öljeitü's death in 1316 aligns with the structure's completion.

events.title: Death of Öljeitü; succession of Abu Sa'id Bahadur Khan in the Ilkhanate
events.description: Öljeitü, the eighth Ilkhanate ruler, died in December 1316 after a reign of 12 years. He was succeeded by his young son Abu Sa'id Bahadur Khan (r. 1316–1335), who was only about 12 years old at accession. During Abu Sa'id's minority, power was exercised by regents and viziers, particularly Amir Chupan. Öljeitü's death marked the beginning of the Ilkhanate's gradual decline; internal factionalism and the rise of regional powers would weaken central authority over the following decades.

sources.name: Jami' al-tawarikh (Compendium of Histories) by Rashid al-Din

events.certainty_note: Öljeitü's death in December 1316 and Abu Sa'id's succession are well-attested in Islamic chronicles. Abu Sa'id's birth year (c. 1304) and age at accession are consistent across sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The Great Famine's severity and mortality rates are debated among historians. Some scholars (e.g., Campbell) argue for lower mortality (2–5%) in some regions, while others (e.g., Benedictow) suggest higher figures (5–10%). The famine's impact varied significantly by region and social class. Additionally, the causal relationship between the famine and the later Black Death is complex: while malnutrition may have weakened populations, the plague's transmission was primarily driven by fleas and rats, not by famine-induced weakness alone. The principle of female exclusion from the French throne, established after Louis X's death, was not universally accepted at the time; some jurists and nobles disputed it, and it took decades to become settled law.
historiographic_note: The year 1316 is well-documented for Western Europe and the Ilkhanate, thanks to contemporary chronicles, royal records, and later compilations. However, sources are heavily weighted toward political and elite events; the experiences of peasants, women, and non-elite populations during the famine are largely invisible in surviving records. For Asia, documentation is more sparse in Western scholarship; Chinese Yuan Dynasty records exist but are less accessible. Sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia are virtually absent from this year's record in English-language sources, reflecting both the limitations of surviving documentation and the Eurocentric bias of historical scholarship.

graph_edges.note: The Great Famine weakened populations and may have contributed to conditions favoring plague transmission.
Louis X's death and the succession crisis directly led to Philip V's coronation.
The exclusion of women from the French succession, formalized in 1316, became a casus belli for the Hundred Years' War.
Öljeitü's death initiated the regency period under Abu Sa'id, which lasted until 1335.

era_context: The early 14th century saw the fragmentation of Mongol unity. The Yuan Dynasty under Ayurbarwada ruled China; the Ilkhanate under Öljeitü dominated Persia; the Golden Horde controlled the Russian steppes. In Europe, the Capetian dynasty ruled France under Louis X; England was under Edward II. This year marks the beginning of the Great Famine (1315–1317), one of the most severe subsistence crises of the medieval period.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Americas (no contemporary European records; indigenous sources largely lost)

events.title: Great Famine of 1315–1317 begins in Northern Europe
events.region: Northern Europe (England, France, Low Countries, Scandinavia, German lands)
events.description: In 1315, prolonged wet weather and cold temperatures caused widespread crop failures across Northern Europe. Grain harvests failed; livestock died from disease and starvation; food prices soared. The famine would persist through 1316 and into 1317, killing an estimated 5–10% of the population in affected regions. This was one of the most severe subsistence crises of the medieval period, with documented cases of malnutrition, disease, and in some areas, cannibalism and infanticide. The famine destabilized feudal economies, triggered social unrest, and weakened populations for the later Black Death.

sources.name: Manorial records and grain price data from England and France
Modern climate reconstructions and demographic studies

events.certainty_note: The Great Famine is well-attested in contemporary manorial records, price data, and later chronicles. The 1315 start date is secure.

events.title: Ayurbarwada (Emperor Renzong) reigns in Yuan Dynasty China
events.description: Ayurbarwada (known in Chinese as Emperor Renzong, r. 1311–1320) continued his reign over the Yuan Dynasty in 1315. His reign was marked by efforts to stabilize the dynasty after internal conflicts and to promote Confucian learning and Han Chinese administrative practices. He attempted to balance Mongol and Han Chinese interests, though his reign was also characterized by factional struggles among Mongol princes and Han Chinese officials. In 1315, the Yuan Dynasty controlled most of East Asia and was economically prosperous, though facing long-term challenges from regional instability and the rise of Han Chinese resistance movements.
key_figures: Confucian scholars and Han Chinese officials

sources.name: Yuanshi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Ayurbarwada's reign dates (1311–1320) are well-documented in the Yuanshi and other Chinese sources.

events.title: Öljeitü rules the Ilkhanate; cultural and architectural patronage
events.description: In 1315, Öljeitü (r. 1304–1316) was in the final year of his reign over the Ilkhanate. He was an active patron of architecture, art, and scholarship, commissioning the Mausoleum of Öljeitü (completed 1316) and supporting the compilation of the Jami' al-tawarikh (Compendium of Histories) by the scholar Rashid al-Din. Öljeitü's court was a center of Islamic learning and artistic production, and his reign represented a high point of Ilkhanate cultural achievement, even as the dynasty faced political fragmentation and economic challenges.

sources.name: Jami' al-tawarikh (Compendium of Histories) by Rashid al-Din
Archaeological evidence of Ilkhanate architecture and art

events.certainty_note: Öljeitü's reign and patronage are well-documented in Islamic chronicles and archaeological evidence.

events.title: Edward II rules England; political instability and baronial opposition
events.description: In 1315, Edward II (r. 1307–1327) was in the eighth year of his troubled reign. He faced strong baronial opposition, particularly from Thomas of Lancaster, who led a coalition of magnates against the king's favorite, Piers Gaveston (executed 1312). Edward II's weak leadership, military failures (especially in Scotland), and dependence on unpopular favorites created chronic instability. In 1315, the Great Famine was beginning to devastate England, adding economic stress to political tensions. Edward II's reign would eventually end in deposition and murder (1327), but in 1315 he remained on the throne, though increasingly isolated.
key_figures: Hugh Despenser the Younger (favorite, later executed)

sources.name: English royal records and parliamentary rolls

events.certainty_note: Edward II's reign and the baronial opposition are well-documented in English chronicles and royal records.

events.title: Battle of Bannockburn aftermath; Scottish independence consolidates
events.description: In 1315, Scotland was consolidating its independence following Robert the Bruce's decisive victory at Bannockburn (1314). Edward II's military failure in Scotland had strengthened Bruce's position and weakened English claims to Scottish overlordship. In 1315, Robert the Bruce was secure on the Scottish throne, and Scotland was beginning to recover from decades of warfare. However, the Great Famine (1315–1317) would soon devastate Scotland as severely as it did England, creating new hardships for the Scottish kingdom.
key_figures: Robert the Bruce (King of Scotland, r. 1306–1329)
Edward II of England (defeated at Bannockburn)
events.certainty_note: The aftermath of Bannockburn and Scottish consolidation are well-documented in chronicles and royal records.

disconfirming_evidence: The Great Famine's causes are debated: while wet weather and cold temperatures are well-attested, some scholars argue that overpopulation and land exhaustion were equally important factors. The famine's severity also varied significantly by region; some areas (particularly southern Europe) were less affected than northern regions. Additionally, the famine's role in weakening populations for the Black Death is contested; the plague's transmission was primarily driven by fleas and rats, not by famine-induced weakness. Edward II's character and abilities have been reassessed by modern historians; some scholars argue that he was not as incompetent as earlier chronicles portrayed, and that baronial opposition was driven more by factional interests than by Edward's inherent unfitness to rule.
historiographic_note: The year 1315 is well-documented for Western Europe, thanks to contemporary chronicles, royal records, and manorial documents. However, sources are heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; the experiences of peasants, women, and non-elite populations are largely invisible. For Asia, documentation is more sparse in Western scholarship; Chinese and Islamic sources exist but are less accessible. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are virtually absent from this year's record in English-language sources.

graph_edges.note: The famine that began in 1315 continued and intensified in 1316.
Edward II's political instability in 1315 contributed to his eventual deposition in 1327.
Scottish independence consolidated after Bannockburn; Robert the Bruce ruled until 1329.

era_context: The early 14th century saw the fragmentation of Mongol unity following Kublai Khan's death (1294). The Yuan Dynasty under Ayurbarwada ruled China; the Ilkhanate under Öljeitü dominated Persia; the Golden Horde controlled the Russian steppes.
